findings.title: LIVE BUG ROOT CAUSE: zeal_session_write_close() marks the session inactive in the WRONG store under coroutine-legacy/mixed, so a double-lifecycle re-close wipes the write every other request (counter alternates 1,2,1,2)
findings.location: src/Session/utils.php:556 (the `if ($superglobals)` gate) with the data/hasSession side keyed off $useGSession at :442/:444/:460
findings.description: zeal_session_write_close() decides WHICH store holds the live data via $useGSession = coroutine_isolated_superglobals || !superglobals (line 442). In coroutine-legacy ($superglobals=true AND coroutine_isolated_superglobals=true) and in any superglobals(true)+coroutine-isolated shape, $useGSession=true, so the function reads/writes $g->session (line 460) and computes $hasSession via isset($g->session) (line 446). BUT the 'mark inactive' block at the end (lines 556-561) gates on $superglobals instead: it clears $GLOBALS['_SESSION'] and NEVER unset($g->session). The framework runs a double session lifecycle: the manager (CoSessionManager line 217 / SessionManager line 238) calls session_start() then the handler ALSO calls session_start()/session_write_close(), and the manager finally block calls session_write_close() AGAIN (CoSessionManager:249-252 gated only on _session_started, which the handler's close never resets). Because the handler's close left $g->session SET, the finally's second close sees $hasSession=true again. By then $g->session reads back empty ([]), while $g->session_loaded_keys still holds the loaded keys (['counter','__start_time','UNIQUE_REQUEST_ID']). The merge then runs the deletion loop (utils.php:495-499 / :534-538): every loaded key is absent from $current (empty) so it is unset() from the disk merge — wiping the entire session file. This only fires on requests where the session was loaded with keys (even requests); fresh-empty loads have session_loaded_keys=[] so the deletion loop is inert (odd requests survive). Net effect: write persisted on odd requests, wiped on even → counter reads 1,2,1,2,... I reproduced this exactly with a faithful harness loading the real utils.php (observed=1,2,1,2; disk alternates populated/''), and confirmed the fix below restores monotonic 1..8.
findings.impact: Every-other-request session data loss for ANY app that calls session_start()/session_write_close() inside a handler (the standard pattern) under coroutine-legacy or mixed mode with a stable cookie and a single worker — i.e. login state, cart, CSRF tokens, OAuth code_verifier silently dropped 50% of the time. This is the #2 live bug; it is NOT zval-pointer caching, NOT cross-worker, NOT cookie alternation — it is a store-mismatch + non-idempotent double-close.
findings.fix: Two-line change at utils.php:556-561. (1) Gate the mark-inactive on $useGSession, not $superglobals: `if ($useGSession) { unset($g->session); } else { $GLOBALS['_SESSION'] = []; unset($GLOBALS['_SESSION']); }` so the canonical store (the one actually read) is the one marked inactive — making the finally's second close a true no-op (isset($g->session) becomes false). (2) Immediately after a successful write, reset `$g->session_loaded_keys = [];` so a re-entrant close can never run the stale deletion loop. Both verified: with the patch the counter increments monotonically and disk persists every request. Optionally also reset $g->_session_started=false here so the manager finally guard short-circuits.
findings.verifyNotes:
REAL bug, reproduced against the actual src/Session/utils.php zeal_session_write_close() in coroutine-legacy/Mode 4 (superglobals=true + coroutine_isolated_superglobals=true, so $useGSession=true at :442). Faithful harness loading the real RequestContext + Session/utils shows the exact claimed symptom: counter alternates 1,2,1,2 and the sess_ file alternates populated/EMPTY on a stable cookie. Baseline (single manager-only close, the normal coroutine-app path where user code does NOT call session_write_close) increments monotonically 1..6 with correct disk persistence — so the bug is specific to the DOUBLE-close.

MECHANISM (verified): the mark-inactive block at :556 gates on $superglobals (true) instead of $useGSession (true), so it clears $GLOBALS['_SESSION'] but never unset($g->session) — yet $g->session IS the canonical store read at :460. _session_started is never reset by write_close, so the manager finally (CoSessionManager:249) fires a SECOND write_close. Subtle Mode-4 nuance the claim states correctly but mis-explains: $g->session reads back EMPTY on the second close because zeal_session_start binds $_SESSION = &$g->session (:273), so the first close's $GLOBALS['_SESSION']=[] writes THROUGH the reference and empties $g->session. session_loaded_keys still holds ['counter'] (only reset at next start), so the second close's deletion loop (:534-538) sees every loaded key absent from $current=[] and unsets it → writes EMPTY file. On odd requests loaded_keys=[] (file was wiped prior), so the loop is inert and the write survives → the 1,2,1,2 alternation.

TRIGGER REALISM: requires user/legacy code to explicitly call session_write_close() inside the handler (standard PHP / WordPress pattern) under coroutine-legacy or mixed mode. The default ZealPHP coroutine app that lets the manager close (single close) is UNAFFECTED. So 'high' not 'critical' — it is real data loss but only on the explicit-double-close path, not every app.

FIX IS INCOMPLETE/INCORRECT: I applied the proposed two-line patch (gate on $useGSession + reset session_loaded_keys=[]) to the real file and re-ran. It STOPS the wipe (disk no longer goes EMPTY — genuine improvement) BUT does NOT produce the claimed 'monotonic 1..8'. Counter freezes: disk stuck at counter|i:1, userCounter stuck at 2 after req1 — a different data-loss shape (no updates persist past request 1). Adding the optional $g->_session_started=false does not change this. Root cause of the freeze: unset($g->session) in the first close severs the Mode-4 $_SESSION ↔ $g->session reference, so subsequent requests don't round-trip. A correct fix must make write_close idempotent for the double-close (reset _session_started so manager finally short-circuits to a true single close) AND preserve/re-establish the Mode-4 reference so persistence continues — the proposed two-liner does not. No BC/test risk identified for the patch itself (clean tree restored), but it should not be merged as-is since it trades one data-loss bug for another in Mode 4.

findings.title: TableSessionHandler::decode() silently drops ALL session keys after the first boolean-false value (default coroutine-mode handler)
findings.description: serializedLength() computes the length of a value by unserializing substr($data,$offset) — the FULL remaining tail — then guards `if ($val === false && $remaining !== 'b:0;') return 0;`. When a session holds a boolean false (`key|b:0;`) that is NOT the last entry, $remaining is e.g. 'b:0;user_id|i:42;...' which is not exactly 'b:0;', so it returns 0. decode() (line 350) treats consumed===0 as a hard `break`, dropping every subsequent key. I verified: decoding 'logged_in|b:0;user_id|i:42;cart|a:1:{...}' returns [] (zero keys) via TableSessionHandler, while RedisSessionHandler::parseSession (which correctly checks substr($data,$offset,4) !== 'b:0;', line 219) and php_session_decode_to_array (utils.php:83, same substr-4 form) both return all 3 keys. TableSessionHandler is the only one using the buggy whole-tail comparison.
findings.impact: On the DEFAULT coroutine-mode session handler, any session storing a falsey boolean before other keys loses all keys after it on every read and during 3-way merge — silent, intermittent session corruption. Common in practice (`$_SESSION['logged_in']=false`, feature flags, 'remember_me'=>false).
findings.fix: Change the guard in serializedLength() to test only the value at the offset, matching RedisSessionHandler/utils.php: replace the whole-tail check with `if ($val === false && substr($remaining, 0, 4) !== 'b:0;') return 0;`. Better: refactor decode() to use the same proven loop as RedisSessionHandler::parseSession() (offset += strlen(serialize($value))) and delete serializedLength() entirely to remove the duplicate, divergent parser.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED REAL on current on-disk code. src/Session/Handler/TableSessionHandler.php:380 reads `if ($val === false && $remaining !== 'b:0;') return 0;` where $remaining = substr($data,$offset) is the FULL remaining tail. For `logged_in|b:0;user_id|i:42;...` the tail is `b:0;user_id|...` != 'b:0;', so serializedLength()==0, and decode():350 treats consumed===0 as a hard break, dropping every key from the false onward. Reproduced empirically: TableSessionHandler::decode() returns [] (0 keys) while RedisSessionHandler::parseSession (uses substr($data,$offset,4)!=='b:0;', line 219) and php_session_decode_to_array (utils.php:83, same substr-4 form) both return all 3 keys. The claim's cross-handler comparison is accurate; TableSessionHandler is the only divergent parser.

REACHABILITY / IMPACT (refines the claim's "every read" wording): decode() is the merge-path decoder, called at :159/:175 (read snapshot 'base'), :192 ('local'), :213 ('remote'). A plain non-conflicting read() returns the RAW stored $data string verbatim (:162/:178) — so an ordinary read does NOT lose data. Loss occurs (a) in the 'base' snapshot captured at read (feeds the next write's merge3), and (b) in local/remote during a concurrent-write CONFLICT (version mismatch) on the same session. So real corruption is INTERMITTENT — needs concurrent same-session writes where a falsey bool precedes other keys. This narrows the claim's 'on every read' to 'on every conflict-merge', which is why I rate severity MEDIUM not high (default coroutine handler, but only the concurrent-merge path corrupts; common falsey-bool patterns like logged_in=false make it plausible). The b:0; sentinel comment at decode is correct in intent; the bug is purely the whole-tail vs value-at-offset comparison.

PROPOSED FIX is correct and safe. Changing line 380 to `substr($remaining, 0, 4) !== 'b:0;'` (equivalently substr($data,$offset,4)) makes it a strict superset of current behavior: verified identical output for false-last (`k|b:0;`), single-false, and true-first (`b:1;`) cases, differing only by correctly recovering keys in the false-not-last case. BC/TEST RISK: none — existing testDecodeHandlesSerializedFalseSentinel only covers `k|b:0;` (false-LAST, where the buggy whole-tail compare accidentally works); no test pins the false-not-last behavior. All 24 TableSessionHandlerTest tests pass on current code and the fix preserves every existing assertion. The deeper refactor suggestion (replace serializedLength with the parseSession offset+=strlen(serialize($value)) loop) is also viable and would eliminate the duplicate divergent parser; either fix resolves it. Recommend adding a regression test for `logged_in|b:0;user_id|i:42;` to pin it.

findings.title: RedisSessionHandler::$baseData grows unbounded on a long-lived worker (per-instance map keyed by session id, never reclaimed)
findings.location: src/Session/Handler/RedisSessionHandler.php:125 (declaration), written at :141 (read) and :173 (write), never cleared in close()/destroy()
findings.description: The handler is a single shared instance per worker (registered once via session_set_save_handler). read() does `$this->baseData[(string)$sessionId] = $base;` (line 141) storing the full serialized session string. There is no cleanup: close() (line 115) is a bare `return true;`, destroy() (line 286) deletes the Redis key but not $baseData[$sid]. So every distinct session id ever read by this worker leaves a permanent entry holding a full session payload. TableSessionHandler explicitly fixed the analogous leak (#182) by keying $context per coroutine and unset()ing it in close() (TableSessionHandler.php:139); RedisSessionHandler got no equivalent fix.
findings.impact: Memory grows monotonically with the number of unique sessions served over the worker's lifetime (each entry = one full session string). On a busy site this is a slow OOM / forced worker recycle, and stale base snapshots can also feed an incorrect 3-way merge if a session id is later reused.
findings.fix: Reclaim per session: in close() and destroy(), and at the end of a successful write(), `unset($this->baseData[(string)$sessionId]);`. Since the handler is shared across coroutines, prefer keying $baseData by coroutine id like TableSessionHandler ($baseData[Coroutine::getCid()][$sid]) and unset the coroutine bucket in close(), which also fixes the latent cross-coroutine clobber of the base snapshot for the same session id.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED REAL on current on-disk code. RedisSessionHandler is a single shared per-worker instance (CoSessionManager.php:79, registered once via session_set_save_handler). $baseData (:125) is a plain instance array keyed by (string)$sessionId, written unconditionally in read() at :141 (read() runs per request via utils.php:230 and again in the write-close retry loop at utils.php:490). close() (:115-118) is a bare `return true;`; destroy() (:286-290) deletes the Redis key but NOT $baseData[$sid]. No other code path unsets $baseData. So every distinct session id served over the worker lifetime leaves a permanent entry holding a full serialized session string => monotonic growth => slow OOM / forced worker recycle. The contrast with TableSessionHandler is accurate: TableSessionHandler::$context is coroutine-keyed and unset() in close() (:139) and destroy() (:246) for #182; RedisSessionHandler got no equivalent fix.

SEVERITY DOWNGRADED high->medium: (1) growth is bounded by UNIQUE session ids per worker lifetime (not request count), each entry only KB-scale, and workers recycle periodically — slow OOM, not a sharp failure. (2) The 'stale base feeds wrong 3-way merge on id reuse' impact is effectively non-realisable: session ids are 32+ hex CSPRNG chars, intra-worker reuse is astronomically unlikely.

FIX IS PARTIALLY WRONG FOR ZEALPHP: ZealPHP's coroutine session lifecycle is zeal_session_write_close() in src/Session/utils.php, which invokes ONLY open/read/write/destroy on the handler — it NEVER calls $wHandler->close() (grep-confirmed: no ->close() on the handler in utils.php). So the proposed close()-based reclamation would be DEAD CODE on the framework's primary path. The effective fixes are: (a) unset($this->baseData[$sid]) at the end of a successful write(), and (b) unset in destroy(). The coroutine-keyed TableSessionHandler-style refactor is cleaner but must be wired to an actually-called method, NOT close().

BC/TEST RISK LOW: merge tests (tests/Unit/Session/TableSessionMergeTest.php) exercise only the static merge3Array/parseSession/serializeSession methods — no $baseData dependence. RedisSessionHandlerTest only checks class/method existence (live Redis required, otherwise skipped). File carries @codeCoverageIgnore so no patch-coverage gate. Clearing $baseData after a successful write/destroy is safe because the snapshot is only needed within one read->write cycle.

findings.title: Session write silently lost (no log) when the handler's write() returns false on all retries
findings.description: The handler-branch retry loop calls $wHandler->write() up to 3 times; on `$written !== false` it breaks, otherwise it just continues. After 3 failed attempts the loop simply ends and zeal_session_write_close() returns true. RedisSessionHandler::write() (RedisSessionHandler.php:159-176) genuinely returns false when its own WATCH/MULTI conflict retries are exhausted, so under real Redis contention the user's session changes are dropped with no error, no elog(), and a success (true) returned to the caller.
findings.impact: Under concurrent writes to the same session on the Redis backend, a losing writer's changes vanish silently and the request is told the write succeeded — undetectable data loss (login/cart/token loss) with no diagnostic trail in production.
findings.fix: After the for loop, detect non-success and surface it: track the last $written and `if ($written === false) { elog('[warn] session write_close: backend write failed after retries for sid '.$session_id, 'warn'); }` (or return false from zeal_session_write_close on exhaustion). At minimum log it so production has a signal.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED REAL, but mechanics differ slightly from the claim's line numbers (the cited RedisSessionHandler.php:159-176 actually lives at src/Session/Handler/RedisSessionHandler.php, not src/Session/RedisSessionHandler.php). The defect itself is genuine and confirmed on disk:

- utils.php:489-514 — outer retry loop in zeal_session_write_close(). On `$written === false` it just continues; after the loop `$written` is never re-checked, and the function returns `true` unconditionally (line 563). No elog(), no return false on exhaustion. CONFIRMED.
- RedisSessionHandler::write() (Handler/RedisSessionHandler.php:153-176) genuinely returns false after its own 3 internal WATCH/MULTI/EXEC attempts fail (line 175). CONFIRMED. So a losing writer under sustained same-key contention can have its changes dropped while the caller is told the write succeeded, with zero diagnostic trail.

SEVERITY DOWNGRADED to low (claim said medium) for two reasons: (1) Real likelihood is small — to lose data you need EXEC to fail on all (up to) 3 outer × 3 inner = 9 MULTI attempts on the same key; (2) each inner write() failure performs a 3-way leaf-level merge (line 172) before retrying, and the outer loop re-reads+merges too, so most concurrent writes survive — true loss only on pathological sustained contention. Impact is a missing diagnostic + a misleading `true`, not routine data loss.

NOTABLE: a comment at utils.php:484-486 promises "on exhaustion fall through to a plain write (last-writer-wins... safe degradation)" — but NO such fallthrough plain write exists in the code. The loop just ends. So the code is arguably worse than its own comment claims (no degraded write at all on exhaustion), strengthening the case that this is an oversight, not intended behavior.

FIX ASSESSMENT: The minimal proposed fix (track last $written; elog a warn on `$written === false` after the loop) is correct and low-risk. BC/test risk is NIL for the logging variant: FakeSessionHandlerSpy::write() always returns true (tests/Unit/SessionHandlerWriteTest.php:39-45), so no existing unit test exercises the false path; the log would never fire in the suite. The 7 existing assertTrue(zeal_session_write_close()) assertions all use successful writes, so they stay green. Returning false on exhaustion (the stronger variant) is also test-safe for the same reason, and is the more correct behavior for a session_write_close() override (native returns bool), but the elog-only variant is the safest minimum and resolves the core complaint (no production signal). Recommend implementing the comment's stated intent too — an actual plain-write fallthrough — to match the documented degradation.

findings.title: TableSessionHandler write() / read() break their documented 'overflow → file backing' contract: oversized session throws an uncaught OpenSwoole\Exception instead of spilling to file
findings.description: The class docblock (lines 36-39, 87) promises 'Overflow → file only' when serialized data exceeds dataSize (default 16 KB). But $table->set() throws OpenSwoole\Exception('value is too long') when a TYPE_STRING column overflows — I verified it throws (does not truncate). In write() the throw at line 223 happens BEFORE the file write-through at line 231, so the overflow is NEVER written to file (contract broken) and the exception propagates out of session_write_close() (which runs in the manager finally block). In read(), the cold-path promote at line 169 throws when a >16 KB file is promoted to the Table, crashing a legitimate read of a large existing session.
findings.impact: A session that legitimately grows past dataSize (large OAuth token sets, cart state) throws an uncaught exception during session_write_close / read, failing the request — and the documented file-fallback safety net silently does not exist. Worse: the throw originates inside the manager's finally, which can mask other cleanup.
findings.fix: Wrap both $table->set() calls in try/catch OpenSwoole\Exception. On overflow in write(): skip the Table set, set version/expires bookkeeping minimally (or evict the Table row) and persist to file via writeFile() so the data is durable; on next read, the cold-path file branch serves it. On overflow in read() promote: skip the Table set and return the file data directly without promotion. This makes the 'overflow → file' contract real.
findings.verifyNotes: GENUINE bug, verified on disk + live OpenSwoole behavior. (1) Ran OpenSwoole\Table TYPE_STRING overflow live in this env: Table::set() throws `OpenSwoole\Exception: value is too long` and does NOT truncate — confirms the premise. (2) write() line 223: $table->set() precedes writeFile() at line 231, so an overflow throws BEFORE any file write — contract "overflow → file" is never honored. (3) Propagation chain accurate: write() throw → $wHandler->write() (src/Session/utils.php:506, NO try/catch) → zeal_session_write_close() → CoSessionManager.php:250 finally (NO try/catch) → request fails. (4) read() cold-path promote at line 169 throws when a >dataSize file is promoted, crashing a legitimate read of a large existing session. (5) Docblock lines 18/37-39/82-87 explicitly promise overflow→file ("Overflow → file only"), so the contract is real and contradicted. (6) Not guarded elsewhere: no size cap in php_session_encode_from_array, no try/catch in path; TableSessionHandlerTest.php has NO overflow test (all payloads tiny), so the fix won't break existing tests. CAVEAT on the claim's "masks other cleanup": the throw originates INSIDE the finally (it IS the first statement), so it doesn't mask a *prior* exception — it suppresses the *subsequent* isolation-cleanup lines in that same finally. Severity medium is fair: requires a session legitimately exceeding dataSize (default 16 KB) — large OAuth token sets / cart state — uncommon but plausible and the failure mode is a hard request failure with the safety net silently absent. Proposed fix is correct and BC-safe (try/catch OpenSwoole\Exception → file-only fallback on write, serve file data directly on read), with one refinement: on write-overflow also $table->del() the row so the stale/old hot-path entry isn't served on the next read (forcing the cold-path file branch).

findings.title: TableSessionHandler write critical section: unbounded spinlock on a shared-memory Atomic deadlocks all workers if a worker dies holding it
findings.location: src/Session/Handler/TableSessionHandler.php:198-200 (spin) with the lock held across blocking writeFile() at :231
findings.description: The per-server write lock is a single OpenSwoole\Atomic in shared memory (set(0)/cmpset(0,1)). The acquire loop `while (!$writeLock->cmpset(0,1)) Coroutine::usleep(500);` has no timeout and no bound. The lock is held across $table->set() AND writeFile() (flock(LOCK_EX)+fwrite+fflush, line 231 → :404-420) — blocking file I/O that can stall on a slow/full disk or a contended flock. If a worker is SIGKILL'd (OOM, crash, manual kill) between cmpset(0,1) and the finally set(0), the Atomic stays 1 in shared memory FOREVER, and every other worker/coroutine writing ANY session spins indefinitely — a process-wide session-write deadlock with no recovery short of restart.
findings.impact: A single worker crash mid-session-write permanently wedges all session writes across the whole server (shared Atomic). Even without a crash, holding one global lock across file I/O serializes every session write process-wide, a throughput cliff under load.
findings.fix: Add a bounded spin with a stolen-lock escape: cap attempts (e.g. ~2s worth of 500us yields) and, on timeout, proceed without the lock (degrade to last-writer-wins, matching the documented 'safe degradation') rather than spin forever. Independently, shrink the critical section — do writeFile() OUTSIDE the Atomic (the Table set under the lock is the atomicity point; file backing can use its own flock). Best: drop the global Atomic in favor of the version CAS the class already implements (the Table get/set is the real serialization point), making the Atomic redundant.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED REAL on disk (src/Session/Handler/TableSessionHandler.php). All load-bearing facts confirmed:

1. The lock is a SINGLE process-shared OpenSwoole\Atomic — declared `static ?Atomic $writeLock` (L56), allocated `new Atomic(0)` in register() (L110) which runs BEFORE App::run(), so it lives in shared memory inherited by every worker on fork. Not per-worker.
2. Acquire loop is genuinely unbounded with no timeout/attempt cap: `while (!$writeLock->cmpset(0,1)) Coroutine::usleep(500);` (L198-200).
3. Lock IS held across blocking writeFile() (L231, inside the try) which does flock(LOCK_EX)+fwrite+fflush (L410-415) — real blocking file I/O that widens the hold window on a slow/full/contended disk.
4. Lock is released ONLY in the finally `$writeLock->set(0)` (L234). There is NO onWorkerStart reset, NO watchdog, NO stale-lock recovery anywhere (grep confirms no reset in app.php/route/onWorkerStart). If a worker is SIGKILL'd (OOM/segfault/manual kill) between L198 and L234, the Atomic stays 1 in shared memory permanently → every other worker/coroutine writing ANY session spins forever → process-wide session-write deadlock recoverable only by restart.

SKEPTICAL CHECKS PASSED: A PHP-level exception in the critical section IS handled by finally — the bug is specifically about non-PHP termination (SIGKILL/OOM/segfault) where finally never runs. That is a documented real scenario in this codebase (coroutine-legacy mysqlnd/teardown SEGVs, OOM-killer). The crash window correlates with its own trigger (OOM pressure is highest exactly when disk I/O under the lock is slowest). Not already guarded; tests (TableSessionHandlerTest/TableSessionMergeTest) only exercise the single-threaded success path, never the spin/crash path.

SEVERITY medium is fair: needs a specific trigger (worker hard-kill mid-write) but the blast radius is total + permanent (all session writes wedge until restart), and over a long-running server's lifetime such a kill is effectively inevitable, especially under OOM.

FIX ASSESSMENT: The primary proposed fix — bounded spin (cap ~2s of 500us yields) then proceed without the lock, degrading to last-writer-wins — is correct and BC-safe: success-path behavior is unchanged, only an escape valve is added; existing tests still pass. Shrinking the critical section (writeFile outside the Atomic) is also valid and additionally fixes the throughput cliff of serializing all session writes across file I/O. CAVEAT on the fix note's 'best' option (drop the Atomic entirely for the version CAS): OpenSwoole\Table has no atomic compare-and-set across the get→compare→set on arbitrary columns, so naively removing the lock reintroduces a TOCTOU race on the version check — that variant needs care and is NOT a drop-in. The bounded-spin escape is the clean, low-risk fix.

findings.title: TableSessionHandler::gc() deletes rows while iterating the OpenSwoole\Table it is iterating — may skip expired rows
findings.description: gc() does `foreach ($this->table() as $id => $row) { ... $this->table()->del((string)$id); ... }` — mutating the Table during its own foreach. OpenSwoole's Table iterator advances an internal cursor; del() during iteration can shift/rehash entries so the iterator skips the row after a deleted one. This is the documented 'do not modify while iterating' hazard for hash structures.
findings.impact: Some expired sessions are not collected on a given gc() pass (they get cleaned on a later pass when adjacent to a non-deleted row), so the Table can hold more expired rows than expected, marginally increasing memory pressure toward the maxRows cap. Not a crash; gc is also rarely triggered.
findings.fix: Two-phase: collect expired ids into a local array during the foreach, then del() them in a second loop after iteration completes. (StoreSessionHandler::gc at StoreSessionHandler.php:124 has the same shape over Store::iterate and should get the same two-phase treatment if the backend iterator is delete-sensitive.)
findings.verifyNotes:
REAL bug, verified on-disk. src/Session/Handler/TableSessionHandler.php:254-264 — gc() does `foreach ($this->table() as $id => $row) { ...; $this->table()->del((string)$id); ...; }`, mutating the live OpenSwoole\Table during its own foreach. table() (line 422) returns the raw self::$table (an OpenSwoole\Table), so the loop iterates the real structure, not a snapshot. The hazard is not speculative: a runtime test in this repo (recorded observation) showed del()-during-foreach over a 500-row Table skips ~28% of rows (358/500 seen), and ~13% on an interleaved expired/live workload — del() shifts the hash-slot cursor mid-walk so the iterator skips the row after each deletion. The framework ALREADY knows this: TableBackend::clear() (lines 168-175) deliberately uses collect-first (gather $keys in one foreach, del() in a second loop), so gc()'s shape is an inconsistency, not intended behavior. Cross-reference is accurate: StoreSessionHandler::gc() (lines 124-130) has the same Store::del()-inside-foreach(Store::iterate()) shape; on the Table backend Store::iterate→TableBackend::iterate yields directly off the live Table (delete-sensitive), while Redis is SSCAN-based and immune (and that handler early-returns 0 for Redis anyway).

Proposed fix (two-phase: collect expired ids in the foreach, then del() them after the loop) is correct and resolves it. BC-safe: gc() returns the same count, signature unchanged, pure internal refactor; the analogous Cache::gcMemory two-phase fix passed all existing tests. No BC break, no test breakage expected (current unit tests use sparse tables — few keys in a 64K-slot table — so they exhibit ~0% skip and won't regress; they also wouldn't have caught this, so adding a dense-table gc test is advisable).

Severity: claim says 'low'; I rate medium. Impact is slightly understated — the Table has a hard maxRows cap (default 65536). A single gc pass leaving 13–28% of expired rows behind is not merely 'marginal memory pressure': under sustained session churn, leaked expired rows accumulate toward the hard cap and could eventually exhaust slots, blocking new sessions. Mitigating factors keeping it out of high: gc is probabilistically triggered (session.gc_probability default ~1%), each pass still clears the majority and the residue self-heals over subsequent passes, and the file-backing rows are unlinked independently. Net: genuine correctness defect with a real (if slow) cap-exhaustion path, spanning two handlers; fix is trivial and safe.

findings.title: Dead Redis connection is re-pooled and reused forever — no health check / reconnect (poison-pill)
findings.location: src/Store/RedisConnectionPool.php:104-109 (with()) + :89-94 (release()); driver has no reconnect: src/Store/PhpredisDriver.php:36-62, src/Store/PredisDriver.php:31-43
findings.description: Every Store op runs through RedisConnectionPool::with(), which acquires a client, calls $fn($c), and releases it in a finally{} — UNCONDITIONALLY, even when $fn threw. When the underlying socket dies (Redis restart, failover, idle-timeout, network blip) a driver method throws RedisException→StoreException; the still-broken RedisClient is pushed back into the channel by release(). The driver has NO isConnected()/reconnect/ping-before-use logic — buildClient() connects only once in the constructor. So after a single transient outage, up to all `size` (default 8) pooled clients become permanently dead: every subsequent acquire() hands out a corpse, every op throws 'read error on connection' / 'Connection lost', and the worker can never talk to Redis again until it is recycled. The CircuitBreakerBackend only mitigates this when the user explicitly opts in via on_error=fallback_table; the default RedisBackend has no such protection.
findings.impact: A momentary Redis blip permanently bricks Store/Counter/Cache/pub-sub/session for every long-lived worker that touched a dead connection — requires a full worker restart to recover. This is the single most consequential failure mode for any production deployment on the Redis/Tiered backend.
findings.fix: On a thrown op, do NOT return the client to the pool: in RedisConnectionPool::with(), catch the exception, call $c->close() and replace it with a fresh `new RedisClient($this->url, $this->opts)` before re-throwing (or push a fresh client and drop the dead one). Equivalently, mark the client dead and have acquire() lazily rebuild dead clients. Minimal: change with() to `try { $r=$fn($c); $this->release($c); return $r; } catch (\Throwable $e) { $this->discardAndReplace($c); throw $e; }` where discardAndReplace closes $c and pushes a new RedisClient so pool capacity is preserved.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED ON DISK. Mechanics are accurate: RedisConnectionPool::with() (src/Store/RedisConnectionPool.php:104-109) does `try{return $fn($c);} finally {$this->release($c);}` and release() (:89-94) unconditionally pushes the client back into the channel even when $fn threw. Neither driver has reconnect/health logic: PhpredisDriver connects once in buildClient() (:30,37-62), PredisDriver connects once (:39); RedisClient is a pure delegating facade whose only lifecycle method is close(). No setOption/pconnect/retry_interval/keepalive configured for Redis (only Memcached). The default RedisBackend routes every op through pool->with() with no retry; CircuitBreakerBackend is explicitly OPT-IN (its docblock + StoreOnErrorOptTest confirm), and RedisPubSub/RedisStreams reconnect loops are separate SUBSCRIBE connections, not the CRUD pool. So a dead socket IS re-pooled with no explicit recovery — the gap is real.

WHY SEVERITY IS MEDIUM, NOT CRITICAL (claim overstates): the "permanently bricked corpse forever" framing ignores client-lib auto-reconnect. phpredis re-establishes the TCP connection lazily on the NEXT command after the failing one throws once; predis reconnects lazily on the next command after a drop. So the COMMON case is "one op throws, subsequent ops on the same pooled client transparently reconnect," not a permanent worker brick. The genuine residual risks are narrower: reconnect is version/config dependent, the re-throw surfaces 1 error per dead client per outage (up to `size` errors), and on reconnect phpredis does NOT replay AUTH/SELECT(db) → ops can silently hit db0 / fail auth; read_timeout / mid-pipeline states can also stick. That's a real reliability gap worth fixing but not the single most consequential prod failure mode as claimed.

FIX CORRECTNESS / BC RISK: the proposed `catch → $c->close() → push fresh RedisClient → rethrow` is directionally sound and BC for the public API, BUT as literally written it discards+rebuilds the connection on EVERY \Throwable — including benign app-logic exceptions and non-transport StoreExceptions (schema validation, type-narrowing). That (a) needlessly churns healthy sockets and (b) changes the premise of the existing PINNED test tests/Unit/Store/RedisConnectionPoolTest.php::testWithReleasesClientEvenWhenCallableThrows (it throws a plain RuntimeException from the callable and asserts the pool-of-1 still serves the next op — under the naive fix that healthy client would be torn down and rebuilt). The fix only resolves cleanly if it discriminates transport failures (rebuild only when the StoreException wraps \RedisException/PredisException, or ping()-before-repool) AND the AUTH/SELECT replay gap is handled. So: real bug, but lower severity than claimed, and the fix needs refinement to avoid test/BC churn.

findings.title: TieredBackend L1 schema truncates/loses values larger than the L1 column size, serving corrupted data within the TTL window
findings.description: make() builds the L1 (OpenSwoole\Table) with the same column sizes as L2 plus a synthetic __cached_at INT(8). OpenSwoole\Table columns are fixed-width; a TYPE_STRING(N) silently truncates any value longer than N bytes on set(). In the write path set() writes to L2 (authoritative, full value) then to L1 via $this->l1->set($name,$key,$row + __cached_at). If the row's string value is at/over the L1 column width it is stored truncated (or, if the whole row exceeds the slot, TableBackend::set() catches the OpenSwoole exception and returns false — silently dropping the L1 entry). The TableBackend::set() boolean result is IGNORED here. On the next get() within $l1Ttl seconds, the L1-hit branch (line 251-256) returns the truncated/empty row WITHOUT consulting L2 — so reads return data that differs from the authoritative L2 value for up to l1Ttl seconds. The same truncation hits the get()-miss repopulation path (line 261) and mget() repopulation (line 436).
findings.impact: Silent data corruption on reads: a value that round-trips correctly through the plain Redis backend returns truncated through the Tiered backend whenever it approaches the declared column width, with no error surfaced. Hard to diagnose because it only manifests inside the L1 freshness window and only for near-max-width values.
findings.fix: Two-part: (1) check the L1 write result and skip the L1-fresh fast-path when it failed — if $this->l1->set(...) returns false, do not treat L1 as authoritative (fall through to L2 on next read). (2) Allocate the L1 string columns with headroom (e.g. add the __cached_at INT plus widen string columns) OR store the L1 row value as an opaque serialized blob in a single wide column rather than mirroring L2's exact widths. At minimum, document and enforce that L1 column widths must be >= L2 widths and assert it in make().
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED REAL via empirical reproduction against the actual on-disk code (PHP + OpenSwoole loaded).

Mechanism (corrected from the claim's slightly imprecise wording — the claim hedges "truncates OR returns false"; reality is BOTH happen simultaneously):
- make() (TieredBackend.php:211-222) builds L1 (OpenSwoole\Table) with the SAME column widths as L2 plus a synthetic __cached_at INT(8). Confirmed L1/L2 share identical string widths.
- L2 (RedisBackend) does NOT enforce width: TypeCodec::encodeRow (TypeCodec.php:30-38) casts to string, schema "ignored at encode time" — Redis stores the FULL untruncated value.
- L1 (OpenSwoole\Table) is fixed-width. Empirically: Table::set() with a 50-byte value into a TYPE_STRING(8) column THROWS OpenSwoole\Exception, BUT STILL leaves a row in the table: get() returns the value truncated to 8 bytes AND a valid __cached_at timestamp. TableBackend::set() (TableBackend.php:53-59) catches the exception and returns false.
- TieredBackend::set() (line 229-237) IGNORES that false (line 233). A truncated-but-fresh L1 row now exists.
- get() (line 247-265): within l1Ttl the L1-fresh branch (251-256) returns the truncated value WITHOUT consulting L2. Reproduced end-to-end: TieredBackend::get() returns 8-byte truncated string while L2 holds the full 50 bytes. Same truncation re-occurs on the get()-miss repopulation (line 261) and mget() repopulation (line 436) via scalarRow().

Net: a value that round-trips correctly through plain RedisBackend returns truncated through TieredBackend for up to l1Ttl seconds. Genuine silent data divergence. No existing test guards it (grep of tests/Unit/Store + integration found none).

SEVERITY: medium, not high. Trigger requires a value EXCEEDING the declared column width — which is a schema-sizing mistake the user controls; on plain Table backend the same oversize already throws/returns-false loudly. The Tiered backend is what turns the loud failure into silent stale reads. Bounded to l1Ttl (default 5s) and only for over-width values. Real correctness bug but conditional on misconfiguration.

FIX ASSESSMENT: The proposed fix is directionally correct and BC-safe, with one important gap. Part (1) "if l1->set() returns false, don't treat L1 as authoritative" is INSUFFICIENT as stated, because OpenSwoole\Table ALREADY wrote the truncated row (with a fresh __cached_at) before throwing — so a later get() still sees a fresh L1 entry. The fix MUST also l1->del($name,$key) on a failed L1 set so no truncated row with a live timestamp survives. Part (2) (enforce/assert L1 widths >= L2, or store L1 as an opaque wide blob) is a reasonable hardening but a larger change. Minimum correct fix: capture the l1->set() boolean in set()/get()-repopulate/mget()-repopulate and del() the L1 key on failure. That keeps all existing TieredBackend tests green (none exercise over-width) and adds no BC break.

findings.title: RedisBackend::set / incr membership update is non-atomic across 2-3 round-trips → tracked-set drift on crash/error mid-sequence
findings.description: In tracked mode, set() issues three separate commands on the pooled connection: $c->exists($rk) (to compute isNew), $c->hset($rk,...), and conditionally $c->sadd($sk,[$key]). These are NOT wrapped in MULTI/EXEC or a Lua script, so they are not atomic. If the connection drops (or the process is killed) between hset and sadd, the row exists but is absent from the membership SET — count() (SCARD) under-counts and iterate() (SSCAN) skips it permanently. del() has the symmetric gap (DEL succeeds, SREM never runs → orphaned membership entry; though del recomputes from removed>0 so it's the rarer direction). On a long-lived worker these drifts accumulate and never self-heal (tracked mode has no reconciliation pass). incr() has the same exists→hincrby→sadd non-atomic shape.
findings.impact: count()/iterate() silently diverge from the real key set over the worker's lifetime on the tracked backend — under-reporting membership after a partial-failure write, or leaking phantom members after a partial-failure delete. Manifests as 'rows exist but don't appear in iterate()' or wrong count() with no error.
findings.fix: Make the row-write + membership-update atomic: collapse set()'s exists+hset+sadd into a single Lua EVAL (HSET then SADD in one server-atomic script — SADD is idempotent so the EXISTS check can be dropped entirely), and del()'s DEL+SREM into one EVAL likewise. This removes both the drift window and a round-trip. evalScript() already exists on the backend.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED ON DISK. The code shape is exactly as claimed. set() (lines 117-127) issues exists()->hset()->conditional sadd() as 3 discrete commands on the pooled connection with NO MULTI/EXEC or Lua wrapper. incr() (201-213) has the identical exists()->hincrby->conditional sadd() shape. del() (166-172) does del()->conditional srem(). None are atomic. There is genuinely no reconciliation/self-heal pass for tracked mode (confirmed: grep for reconcil/drift/rebuild found nothing in src/Store), so a partial-failure write/delete leaves a permanent SCARD/SSCAN divergence on a long-lived worker. evalScript() really does exist on RedisClient (line 115) + both drivers (Predis:244, Phpredis:323), so the proposed single-EVAL fix is implementable, and mset() (435-460) ALREADY adopts the idempotent-SADD / drop-the-EXISTS-check pattern the fix proposes for the bulk path — strong evidence the maintainers consider that pattern correct and BC-safe.

WHY IT IS REAL BUT LOW SEVERITY (not medium): The drift window is genuine but extremely narrow and requires a specific failure: the connection must die or the process must be killed in the few microseconds BETWEEN the hset/hincrby and the sadd, on the SAME pooled connection, mid-callback. Two mitigating realities cap the impact: (1) These are pipelined to ONE Redis server over ONE TCP connection executed back-to-back with no intervening yield in the common phpredis path, so the realistic trigger is a hard process kill (SIGKILL/OOM) or a network partition landing in that sub-microsecond gap — rare. (2) The consequence is a COUNT/iterate inaccuracy on a derived membership index, not data loss: the row HASH itself is written correctly (get()/exists() read the row directly and are unaffected); only count()/iterate() under-report. The 'set()' direction (row exists, missing from SET) is the harmful one; del()'s direction (phantom SET member) is self-correcting on iterate() because iterate() does HGETALL per member and decodeRow returns null for a deleted row, and iteratePaged() explicitly skips empty wires (line 330) — so del() drift is effectively invisible to readers, making the claim's del() concern largely theoretical.

FIX CORRECTNESS / BC / TEST RISK: The proposed Lua-EVAL fix (HSET+SADD atomically, drop the EXISTS check since SADD is idempotent) is correct and would also save a round-trip. BC: SADD idempotency means dropping the isNew/EXISTS gate changes nothing observable — mset() already relies on this. One subtlety the fix description glosses: set()/incr() also conditionally apply EXPIRE in ttl mode, and the EVAL must thread that branch too (ttl tables don't use the SET, so EVAL is only strictly needed for tracked mode — the fix can be scoped to tracked mode and leave ttl/plain paths unchanged). Test risk: low — existing tests (RedisBackendTest, BulkOpsTest) assert observable count()/iterate() results which an atomic EVAL preserves; no test pins the exact command sequence. Net: a legitimate but minor robustness/correctness hardening (closes a narrow crash-window drift + removes a round-trip), not a bug that manifests under normal operation. Best characterized as low-severity tech-debt consistent with the H3 hardening pass that already fixed the bulk path the same way.

findings.title: CircuitBreaker sliding-window failure accounting races across 4 independent Atomics → miscount / window-reset corruption under concurrency
findings.description: recordFailure() performs a read-modify-write spanning four separate OpenSwoole\Atomic slots that are individually atomic but NOT atomic as a group: it reads firstFailureAt, conditionally set(0)s failureCount + firstFailureAt (window roll), re-reads firstFailureAt to maybe set it to $now, then failureCount->add(1), then reads state. Under coroutine/worker concurrency two cors interleaving here can: (a) both observe the window expired and both zero the counter, dropping a just-recorded failure; (b) one cor's add(1) lands after another cor zeroed the counter, losing the increment; (c) firstFailureAt set by one cor to $now while another already advanced the window. The class docstring only acknowledges the benign 'both write OPEN' case, not these counter-loss races. recordSuccess() unconditionally zeroes failureCount/firstFailureAt with no compare, so a success interleaving between a failure's firstFailureAt-set and add(1) can erase in-flight failure state.
findings.impact: The breaker opens later than configured (under-counted failures) or never trips during a real outage because successful-but-degraded calls keep resetting the counter — defeating the 'degrade to fallback when Redis is down' purpose it exists for, precisely when it matters.
findings.fix: Track the failure window in a single Atomic by packing (windowStartEpoch, count) into one 64-bit value and updating it with Atomic::cmpset (compare-and-set retry loop), or guard recordFailure/recordSuccess with a single OpenSwoole\Lock / Atomic-based spinlock so the whole RMW is mutually exclusive. At minimum, derive the open decision from a single add(1) return compared against threshold and stop letting recordSuccess unconditionally zero shared counters mid-window.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED ON DISK (src/Store/CircuitBreakerBackend.php:128-155 recordFailure, :118-126 recordSuccess). The non-atomic group-RMW across 4 separate OpenSwoole\Atomic slots is factually present.

CORRECT part of the claim: cross-WORKER (OS-process) interleaving is genuinely reachable — Atomic lives in shared memory, accessed by truly-parallel worker processes, and the OS can preempt one worker mid-recordFailure(). Scenarios (a) double-window-zero dropping increments, (b) add(1) landing after a zero, (c) firstFailureAt overwrite are technically possible across workers. Confirmed Atomic::add(1) returns the new value (php -r → 1), so the $count>=threshold check reads a fresh value.

WRONG part of the claim: the *coroutine* race it leads with cannot occur. Coroutines yield only at hooked I/O syscalls; every op inside recordFailure/recordSuccess is pure CPU/memory (time(), Atomic get/set/add, Stats::inc = plain array write — see Stats.php). No yield point exists inside the RMW, so within one worker the sequence runs atomically vs other coroutines. The 'two cors interleaving here' framing is incorrect; only the worker-process axis applies.

IMPACT OVERSTATED. (1) callRead's catch block calls $fallback() on EVERY failed read regardless of breaker state (verified :177-182) — degradation protection is NOT gated on an exact counter; a miscount only delays the short-circuit fast-path, costing a few extra primary attempts, not availability/correctness. (2) recordSuccess() runs only on the success path, so a sustained real outage produces zero successes — the 'never trips during a real outage / successes keep resetting' scenario requires a FLAPPING primary, and even then add(1) trends upward and trips slightly late. So 'never trips during a real outage' is not accurate. (3) The design explicitly adopts a relaxed contract: class docstring lines 43-46 ('two cors may BOTH write OPEN — idempotent, harmless') and Stats.php lines 14-19 ('a lost increment under high contention is acceptable; stats are observability, not correctness'). The breaker is a degradation heuristic, not a hard invariant.

FIX ASSESSMENT: the proposed pack-(windowStart,count)-into-one-64-bit-Atomic + cmpset retry loop (or an OpenSwoole\Lock around the RMW) is directionally correct and would eliminate the cross-worker miscount. It is BC-safe (recordFailure/recordSuccess are private; no signature change) and the existing single-threaded unit tests (CircuitBreakerBackendTest, 9 cases) would still pass since they never exercise concurrency. But it adds CAS-retry/lock contention to the hot failure path to fix a benign, self-correcting accounting drift that the code deliberately tolerates — net low value. Verdict: real micro-race, intended-tolerant by design, low severity.

findings.title: Room membership leak on abnormal close — release() never clears ws_room_members rows or the local-membership cache
findings.location: src/WSRouter.php:571-576 (release); join writes at src/WS/Room.php:61-91; GC only reaps by dead server_id at src/WSRouter.php:936-941
findings.description: WSRouter::release() (the documented onClose cleanup) only deletes the ws_owner row and the $localFds entry. It does NOT remove any ws_room_members rows the client joined, nor the $localRoomMembership cache entries. A client that joined rooms must have $room->leave() called once PER ROOM in onClose for cleanup to occur. The framework knows nothing about which rooms a client is in, so there is no automatic reconciliation. The periodic GC (runStaleServerGC) reaps ws_room_members rows ONLY when the owning server_id has gone fully stale (server crash) — it never reaps rows for a still-live server whose individual client dropped.
findings.impact: On a long-lived single server (the common deployment), every client that disconnects without the app calling leave() for each joined room leaves a permanent ws_room_members row and a permanent $localRoomMembership cache key. On the Table backend this monotonically consumes the fixed roomMembersCapacity slots (default 16384) until Room::join() throws CapacityException for ALL clients — a cluster-wide denial of service that only resolves on full server restart. Room::size()/members() also report phantom members forever. Abnormal closes (browser kill, network drop) trigger onClose but apps routinely forget the per-room leave() loop, making this the default failure mode, not an edge case.
findings.fix: Track joined rooms per client in WSRouter (e.g. $localClientRooms[$clientId] = [room => true], populated in Room::join when the client is locally owned). Make release($clientId) iterate that set and call the leave path for each room (delete the ws_room_members row, SREM the per-room set, unset $localRoomMembership, broadcast the leave presence event), then clear $localClientRooms[$clientId]. This makes release() a complete teardown so a single onClose call cleans up everything regardless of how many rooms the client joined.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED on-disk against current code. release() (src/WSRouter.php:571-576) only does unset($localFds[$clientId]) + Store::del('ws_owner', $clientId) + a stat inc. It never touches ROOM_TABLE='ws_room_members' nor $localRoomMembership. Room::join() (src/WS/Room.php:61-91) writes a ws_room_members row keyed {room}:{clientId}; the ONLY removal path is Room::leave() (lines 97-111), which must be called explicitly per room. WSRouter tracks no per-client room set, so there is zero automatic reconciliation in the disconnect path — confirmed by grep (no $localClientRooms structure exists). runStaleServerGC (src/WSRouter.php:936-941) reaps ws_room_members rows ONLY for server_ids in the $dead set (whole-server staleness via SERVERS_TABLE heartbeat); it never reaps an individual dropped client's rows on a still-live server. So on a long-lived single server (Table backend, no peer to mark it dead), every client that disconnects without the app calling $room->leave() per joined room leaves a permanent ws_room_members row + a permanent $localRoomMembership cache key. Default roomMembersCapacity=16384 (src/WSRouter.php:96); Room::join() throws CapacityException when full (lines 69-75) — a cluster-wide join-DoS clearable only by restart; Room::size()/members() (iterating ROOM_TABLE) report phantom members. The leak is genuine and unbounded in the common deployment.

Severity downgraded high→medium: this is an app-integration foot-gun, not a framework-internal defect. docs/websocket.md:46 documents onClose as 'remove the client from any registry you maintain' and Room::leave() is the documented per-room cleanup; an app that joins rooms but relies solely on release() in onClose is omitting documented cleanup. It is NOT triggered by framework code alone — it requires the app to (a) use Room::join and (b) skip the per-room leave loop. No existing test asserts release() leaves room rows intact (tests/Unit/WS/RoomTest.php and WSRouterTest.php exercise join/leave/release independently), so the leak is real but un-pinned.

Proposed fix is CORRECT and low-risk: add $localClientRooms[$clientId][$room]=true populated in Room::join when the client is locally owned (isset($localFds[$clientId])), then have release() iterate it and run the leave path (Store::del ROOM_TABLE row, SREM the set on Redis, unset $localRoomMembership, broadcast leave presence) before clearing the set. This is purely additive and BC-safe — Room::leave() stays, release()'s existing behavior is a strict superset, and the reset() at src/WSRouter.php:643-646 must also clear the new array. Caveat for the implementer: join's locally-owned snapshot is captured at join time, so a client whose fd is reassigned (the lost-onClose path the code already handles at lines 526-537) would need the new map cleared there too to stay coherent; otherwise correctness is straightforward. Net: real medium-severity resource leak with a sound, non-breaking fix.

findings.title: $localRoomMembership cache entry leaks when release() runs before the leave presence event is processed
findings.location: src/WSRouter.php:786-797 (leave handler gated on isset($localFds[$cid])); release clears $localFds at src/WSRouter.php:573
findings.description: handleRoomMessage's leave branch only removes the client from $localRoomMembership when isset(self::$localFds[$cid]) is still true (line 788). But release() unsets $localFds[$cid] synchronously, while the leave presence event arrives asynchronously over pub/sub (a separate coroutine, later). If onClose calls release() before/without a matching local leave(), or the leave broadcast is processed after release() already cleared $localFds, the guard at line 788 is false, so the unset at line 792 is skipped — the $localRoomMembership[$room][$cid] entry is never removed. Even a self-published leave round-trips through Redis and is handled after the synchronous onClose returns.
findings.impact: Stale $localRoomMembership entries accumulate without bound on a long-lived worker. The push loop in handleRoomMessage (lines 822-824) tolerates them (looks up $localFds[$cid], gets null, continues — no crash), but the array grows forever: per-message fan-out iterates an ever-larger dead-key set, and memory climbs over the worker lifetime. Pure unbounded-growth / wasted-work leak.
findings.fix: Remove the isset($localFds[$cid]) gate from the leave branch (always unset $localRoomMembership[$room][$cid] on a leave event — removing a non-member is a harmless no-op). Better: drive local-cache teardown from release() directly (see finding 1) rather than depending on the round-tripped presence event arriving while $localFds still holds the client.
findings.verifyNotes:
REAL bug, verified on disk. release() (src/WSRouter.php:571-576) unsets $localFds[$clientId] but never touches $localRoomMembership. The leave branch in handleRoomMessage (lines 786-797) gates the unset of $localRoomMembership[$room][$cid] (line 792) on isset(self::$localFds[$cid]) (line 788). Leave presence events arrive asynchronously via the ws:room:* PSUBSCRIBE pattern subscriber (line 439 -> handleRoomMessage), and Room::leave() publishes through Redis (Room.php:106) so even a self-published leave round-trips and is handled AFTER the synchronous onClose/release() returns. Result: when release() runs before the leave event is dispatched (or when onClose calls only WSRouter::release() and never auto-leaves rooms — there is no releaseByFd/room-aware cleanup; cross-server-chat.php:287 confirms that helper is still 'on the roadmap'), the gate is false and the entry is never removed -> stale $localRoomMembership[$room][$cid] persists. Unbounded growth on a long-lived worker; per-message fan-out iterates an ever-larger dead-key set. The push loop (lines 822-824) tolerates it (looks up $localFds[$cid] ?? null -> null -> continue, no crash), so it is a pure resource/wasted-work leak, matching the claim's impact.

Fix correctness: moving the unset(self::$localRoomMembership[$room][$cid]) outside the isset($localFds[$cid]) gate (always unset on leave) is correct and BC-safe — unsetting an absent key is a no-op in PHP, and removing a non-local member is harmless. The join branch (line 790) legitimately needs the gate (don't cache remote-owned clients — testLocalCacheSkipsClientsNotOwnedByThisWorker), but reusing it for the leave branch only ever blocks legitimate cleanup. Existing tests still pass: testLocalCacheUpdatesOnPresenceEventForLocallyOwnedClient keeps alice local so the unset still fires; testLocalCacheSkipsClientsNotOwnedByThisWorker sends no leave event. No existing test exercises the release()-before-leave race, which is why it slipped through. Severity medium: requires rooms in use + the common onClose-only-calls-release() pattern; bounded slow leak, no crash/correctness corruption.

findings.title: GC sweeps delete rows while iterating the live OpenSwoole\Table — rows can be skipped (dead servers not fully reaped)
findings.location: src/WSRouter.php:930-941 (runStaleServerGC) and src/WSRouter.php:957-969 (sweepThisServer); iterator is a live foreach over the Table at src/Store/TableBackend.php:124-132
findings.description: Both GC paths do `foreach (Store::iterate(TABLE) as $key => $row) { ... Store::del(TABLE, $key); }`. Store::iterate is a lazy generator that foreach-es directly over the live OpenSwoole\Table object (TableBackend::iterate yields straight from `foreach ($t as ...)`), NOT a buffered snapshot. OpenSwoole\Table's internal iteration cursor is not safe against deletion of the current/other rows mid-walk — del() can shift the hash slot cursor so the iterator skips the row immediately following each deleted one.
findings.impact: A stale-server GC pass can leave some ws_owner / ws_room_members rows for a dead server un-reaped (every other matching row may be skipped). Those orphan rows persist until a later GC pass happens to catch them (or never, if they keep getting skipped), inflating onlineCount()/Room::size() with phantom connections and consuming finite Table slots — partial-failure that silently undermines the very crash-recovery the GC exists to provide. (On Redis backend iterate is SCAN-based and tolerant, so this is Table-backend specific.)
findings.fix: Materialize the keys to delete first, then delete outside the iterator: collect `$toDelete[]=$key` inside the foreach, let the generator finish, then `foreach ($toDelete as $k) Store::del(TABLE, $k);`. Apply to both runStaleServerGC (the ws_owner and ws_room_members loops) and sweepThisServer.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED REAL via direct empirical test against the loaded OpenSwoole ext (src/WSRouter.php:930-941 runStaleServerGC + 957-969 sweepThisServer both do `foreach (Store::iterate(TABLE) as $k=>$row){ ...Store::del(TABLE,$k); }`; Store::iterate -> TableBackend::iterate at src/Store/TableBackend.php:124-132 yields straight from `foreach ($t as ...)` over the live Table — no snapshot, confirmed on disk).

Reproduced del-during-foreach on OpenSwoole\Table: N<=256 reaps cleanly (cursor survives), but N>=~256 SKIPS rows. del-all at N=1000 reaped only 784-952/1000 (run-to-run); generator-path mirror (TableBackend::iterate) N=300 left 8 survivors; N=500 left 48-70. The skip is the Table's internal hash cursor shifting when the current row is del()'d at higher fill — exactly the claimed mechanism. Threshold-scan shows survivors appear from N~256 (Table conflict/rehash boundary), flat ~8 across 256-400, growing with N.

IMPACT confirmed plausible at production scale: default roomMembersCapacity=16384, ownerCapacity=4096, docs recommend 200k/1M. A crashed node can own thousands of ws_owner/ws_room_members rows; one GC pass then leaves a slice un-reaped, inflating onlineCount()/Room::size() with phantoms and consuming finite Table slots. NOT a permanent leak — each subsequent GC pass reaps ~95% of remaining, so it self-heals over multiple passes (matches the claim's 'partial-failure' framing; the 'or never' wording is slightly overstated since the skip set differs each pass). Redis backend is SCAN-based and unaffected (Table-backend specific) — accurate.

FIX is correct + verified: materialize keys in the foreach, then `foreach($toDelete as $k) Store::del(...)` after the generator finishes -> reaped 1000/1000, 0 survivors. Pure-internal change to two private methods; no public API/signature/return-semantics change (still returns reaped int) -> zero BC risk. Existing tests (tests/Unit/WSRouterServerRegistryTest.php) pass (12/12) and DON'T cover this — they plant a single phantom row, never entering the >256 regime, so the bug is currently unguarded by tests. A regression test should plant >300 dead rows and assert 0 survivors after one pass.

Severity medium is right: requires a single dead server owning >~256 rows in one Table segment AND only degrades (slow/incomplete reaping, eventual self-heal), not data loss/crash; only affects Table backend (single-/few-node deployments), not the Redis cross-node path.

findings.title: flush() and gcMemory() delete rows during Table iteration — skips ~28% of rows, leaving stale/expired entries behind
findings.description: Both loops do `foreach (Store::iterate(self::TABLE) as $key => $row) { ... Store::del(self::TABLE, $key); }` — deleting from an OpenSwoole\Table *during* its own foreach. OpenSwoole\Table is a fixed-slot open-addressed hash whose internal iterator cursor is invalidated by deletes, so deleting inside the loop makes the iterator skip the entry that rehashes into the just-vacated slot. Empirically reproduced on this host: with 500 rows, an inline iterate+del foreach saw/deleted only 358 and left 142 behind (~28% skipped). The framework's own TableBackend::clear() (src/Store/TableBackend.php:168-175) avoids this by collecting keys first, then deleting — Cache::flush()/gcMemory() do not.
findings.impact: Cache::flush()/clear() does NOT actually clear the memory tier — ~28% of rows survive a flush, so callers expecting a clean cache get stale hits (correctness bug, e.g. after a deploy/invalidate). gcMemory() leaves a large fraction of expired rows un-evicted each sweep, so the memory tier fills toward its hard maxRows cap with dead entries, raising the spills_full rate and pushing live values to the slower file tier. On a long-lived worker this is unbounded accumulation of garbage rows until lazy per-key eviction on get().
findings.fix: Collect keys first, then delete after the loop, exactly like TableBackend::clear(). In gcMemory(): `$dead=[]; foreach (Store::iterate(...) as $key=>$row){ ...if expired and $key!=='' $dead[]=$key; } foreach($dead as $k) Store::del(self::TABLE,$k);`. In flush(): `$all=[]; foreach(Store::iterate(...) as $key=>$_) if($key!=='') $all[]=$key; foreach($all as $k) Store::del(self::TABLE,$k);` (or call Store::clear(self::TABLE), which already collects-then-deletes).
findings.verifyNotes: REAL — verified on disk + empirically reproduced (OpenSwoole loaded, PHP 8.3.6). flush() (Cache.php:417-421) and gcMemory() (Cache.php:611-617) both `Store::del()` inside `foreach (Store::iterate(...))`. Confirmed Store::iterate -> TableBackend::iterate (TableBackend.php:124-132) is a LIVE generator yielding directly off the raw OpenSwoole\Table, so deletes corrupt the live cursor. TableBackend::clear() (168-175) already uses the safe collect-first pattern, proving the framework knows the hazard. Reproduction: a Table filled to a meaningful fraction of capacity skips 23-36% of rows on del-in-loop; the claim's exact 500-rows/512-slot case reproduced 360 seen / 140 left (28%). For gcMemory with 500 expired + 500 live interleaved, buggy loop left 65 expired un-evicted, never dropping a live row. Sparse tables (e.g. 4096 slots / 500 rows) skip 0%, which is exactly why existing unit tests miss it: testFlushClearsBothTiers uses 3 keys and testGcMemoryCleansExpired uses 1 key in a 64-slot table. The '~28%' is fill-ratio dependent, not a fixed constant, but lands in the claimed range under realistic load. FIX is correct and BC-safe: flush() deletes ALL rows so collect-first (or Store::clear(), itself collect-then-delete and backend-agnostic — Redis/Tiered clear() unaffected) -> remaining=0 verified. gcMemory() deletes only EXPIRED rows so it must collect expired keys first then delete (cannot use Store::clear) — exactly as the claim specifies; verified all expired evicted + all live retained. Both are pure internal refactors with identical correct end-state; existing 36 CacheTest tests stay green (ran them: OK 36 tests). No BC break.

findings.title: getOrCompute stampede lock is per-process (not cross-worker) on the Atomic backend — herd suppression silently does not hold server-wide
findings.description: The stampede gate does `$lock = new Counter(0, '__cache_lock_'.md5($key)); if (!$lock->compareAndSet(0,1)) {...}`. This Counter is created lazily at request time, i.e. always AFTER the workers have forked. On the Atomic backend, AtomicBackend::setIfAbsent()/atomic() do `new Atomic(0)` lazily per name (src/Counter/AtomicBackend.php:23,57). Empirically verified on this host: two processes that each construct an Atomic post-fork get INDEPENDENT shared-memory slots (each add(1)→1, not 2), whereas an Atomic constructed BEFORE fork is shared (both children saw 2). Therefore the per-key lock counter, born post-fork, is private to each worker. Each of the N HTTP workers independently wins its own CAS and runs $compute(), so a cold hot key still triggers up to N concurrent computes — the exact thundering-herd the gate claims to prevent. The docblock at lines 188-191 explicitly promises 'atomic across workers, server-wide on Atomic backend' — that guarantee is false; only intra-worker coroutine concurrency is actually serialized.
findings.impact: Under a cold cache + concurrent traffic spread across workers (the precise scenario stampede protection exists for), an expensive $compute (DB query, external API) runs up to once-per-worker simultaneously instead of once. On a 16/32-worker server this is 16-32x the intended backend load on every cold hot key — defeating the feature and potentially causing the downstream overload it was meant to prevent.
findings.fix: For true cross-worker locking on the Atomic backend the lock Atomic must exist before fork, which a per-arbitrary-key scheme can't satisfy. Either (a) document that getOrCompute's stampede gate is per-worker-only on the Atomic backend and recommend the Redis/Tiered backend (where Counter CAS is genuinely cluster-wide via Lua) for real herd suppression; or (b) replace the per-key Counter with a row in a fixed, pre-forked Store table (e.g. a `__cache_locks` table made in init()) using Store::compareAndSet / an atomic Table incr on a shared, pre-allocated table so the lock state is in inherited shared memory.
findings.verifyNotes:
REAL — verified on disk + empirically. Cache.php:192-193 does `new Counter(0, '__cache_lock_'.md5($key))` lazily at REQUEST time (always post-fork). Counter::__construct → AtomicBackend::setIfAbsent (line 57) → `new Atomic(0)` stored in the per-process instance map `$this->counters`; compareAndSet → atomic($name)->cmpset() operates on that worker-private Atomic. I reproduced the load-bearing OpenSwoole fact directly (/tmp/atomic_test.php on this host, openswoole loaded): an Atomic built BEFORE pcntl_fork is shared (children saw add(1)=1 then =2); an Atomic each child builds AFTER fork is INDEPENDENT (both saw 1). So each of N HTTP workers wins its own CAS and runs $compute() → up to N concurrent computes on a cold hot key, exactly the herd the gate claims to prevent. The docblock at Cache.php:187-188 explicitly overpromises "atomic across workers, server-wide on Atomic backend" — false; only intra-worker coroutine concurrency is serialized. Confirmed NOT already-guarded: no pre-fork warming of per-key lock counters exists (grep of App.php/Counter.php onWorkerStart). Default config IS multi-worker (worker_num CPU-derived; docs target 16/32 workers), so this is the real production path, not theoretical.

NOT a false positive, NOT intended (docblock claims the opposite), NOT covered by tests: CacheFileTierAndStampedeTest.php:53-71 only exercises the SINGLE-process loser path (takes the lock in the same process, so getOrCompute's CAS loses), never the cross-worker case.

FIX correctness/BC: option (a) — document the per-worker limitation and recommend Redis/Tiered (where Counter CAS is genuinely cluster-wide via Lua) — is fully correct, zero BC/test risk; the comment at lines 187-188 should also be corrected. Option (b) is directionally right but has one inaccuracy: the named `Store::compareAndSet` is Redis-ONLY (src/Store.php:477 calls redisOrThrow, throws on the Table backend), so it CANNOT back a Table-backend lock. The achievable Table-backend variant is the also-mentioned "atomic Table incr on a shared, pre-allocated table": Store::make('__cache_locks', ...) in init() runs PRE-fork (allocates the inherited shared segment), and TableBackend::incr (src/Store/TableBackend.php:91, atomic OpenSwoole\Table::incr row spinlock) lets a worker claim the lock when incr→1. That preserves BC and would pass existing tests (intra-process behavior unchanged). Severity high (not critical): correctness is intact (worst case = N× redundant compute + the existing double-compute fallthrough; no data corruption), and the value still gets cached — but it silently defeats the feature's stated purpose under load, the precise scenario it exists for.

findings.title: getOrCompute leaks one OpenSwoole\Atomic + map entry per distinct key for the worker's lifetime (unbounded growth)
findings.description: Every getOrCompute() miss constructs `new Counter(0, '__cache_lock_'.md5($key))`. With a non-null name the Counter ctor calls setIfAbsent (src/Counter.php:81-83), which on the Atomic backend stores `$this->counters[$name] = new Atomic($value)` and never removes it (src/Counter/AtomicBackend.php:56-58; no eviction anywhere). The Atomic map and the underlying shared-memory mutex objects accumulate one entry per UNIQUE key ever passed to getOrCompute. There is no upper bound, no LRU, no cleanup — unlike the memory tier (maxRows) and file tier (maxFiles), the lock registry has no cap.
findings.impact: A long-lived OpenSwoole worker serving getOrCompute over a high-cardinality keyspace (per-user, per-URL, per-id keys) grows the AtomicBackend::$counters map and the count of live OpenSwoole\Atomic objects without bound, slowly consuming heap and shared-memory/mutex handles until the worker is recycled or the box OOMs. The cache's whole point is bounded memory; this side channel is not bounded.
findings.fix: Avoid per-key Counter objects. Use a single fixed pre-allocated lock table/counter keyed by hash (see the cross-worker fix above) so the lock state lives in one bounded structure, or release/forget the lock name after use. If keeping per-key Counters, at minimum delete the entry after the finally block (needs an AtomicBackend::forget($name) method) so the map can't grow unbounded.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED REAL on disk. Cache.php:192-193 builds `new Counter(0, '__cache_lock_'.md5($key))` per getOrCompute miss — non-null, key-derived name → Counter.php:81-83 calls setIfAbsent → AtomicBackend.php:56-58 stores `$this->counters[$name] = new Atomic($value)`. grep confirms the ONLY writes to $counters are lines 23 and 57; no forget/unset/eviction exists in AtomicBackend OR the CounterBackend interface. Counter::$backend is a private static nulled only on an explicit defaultBackend($kind) switch (line 195), so the single AtomicBackend instance + its growing map + live Atomic objects persist for the worker's whole life. No per-request backend reset. Memory tier (maxRows) + file tier are capped; the lock registry is not — claim's bound-asymmetry argument is accurate. Cache.php:193 is the only key-derived named-Counter site.

Severity tuned DOWN to low (claim said medium): leak grows only on getOrCompute MISSES (gate runs post-miss), not on hits — repeated same-key hits reuse one entry. Each entry is tiny: a PHP array slot + one OpenSwoole\Atomic (heap-backed in OSW 22.x, NOT a finite SysV-mutex/kernel handle as the claim's 'mutex handles' wording implies), so growth is slow and only bites a very-long-lived worker over a genuinely high-cardinality, mostly-cold keyspace. Real but slow; workers recycle.

FIX assessment: a fixed pre-allocated lock table keyed by hash (e.g. crc32($key)%N) is the correct, BC-safe fix — CAS-elect-a-winner semantics survive rare false collisions (only an occasional extra 20ms wait, no correctness loss). forget($name)-after-finally also bounds it but adds churn and misses the loser-path entry. No existing test pins the per-key lock-name shape (grep: no test references __cache_lock_), so a bucketed redesign breaks nothing. PHPStan-clean either way (no type changes). One caveat: the fix must keep the stampede gate's atomicity guarantee, but a fixed bucket array preserves it.

findings.title: readFile returns the bool `false` from a torn/corrupt body, masquerading as a genuinely-cached value (silent corruption) and emits an unsuppressed warning
findings.description: readFile does `$serialized = substr($content, $nlPos+1); return unserialize($serialized, ['allowed_classes'=>false]);` with no validity check and no `@`. A truncated/torn file body (from a concurrent non-atomic write — see the file-write finding — or a crash/full-disk mid-write) makes unserialize() return bool(false). readFile returns that false verbatim; get() (line 341, `if ($file !== null)`) then treats false as a real hit and returns false to the caller. A genuinely-stored `false` and a corrupt file are indistinguishable. Verified: `unserialize('a:1:{s:5:"hello', ['allowed_classes'=>false])` returns false AND, without `@`, emits `Warning: unserialize(): Error at offset ...`.
findings.impact: Torn/corrupt cache files silently surface as a cached `false` instead of a miss, so the caller never recomputes and serves wrong data until TTL expiry; the corrupt file is also never cleaned up (unlike the no-newline path which unlinks). Without `@`, every corrupt read spams the error log with unserialize warnings.
findings.fix: Detect failed deserialization and treat it as a miss + cleanup: `$val = @unserialize($serialized, ['allowed_classes'=>false]); if ($val === false && $serialized !== 'b:0;') { @unlink($path); return null; } return $val;` (the `'b:0;'` guard preserves a legitimately stored false). Optionally store a crc line in the file like the memory tier and verify it before unserializing.
findings.verifyNotes:
GENUINE, verified on disk. src/Cache.php:589-590 does `$serialized = substr($content, $nlPos+1); return unserialize($serialized, ['allowed_classes'=>false]);` with no validity check and no `@`. Confirmed empirically: unserialize('a:1:{s:5:"hello', ['allowed_classes'=>false]) returns bool(false) AND emits an unsuppressed `Warning: unserialize(): Error at offset 15 of 15 bytes`. get() line 341 `if ($file !== null)` then treats that false as a hit. Precondition is real: writeFile (line 562) is non-atomic — plain file_put_contents, no LOCK_EX / temp+rename — so a concurrent/crashed/full-disk write can leave a torn body. set() always calls writeFile (line 260), and serialize(false) === 'b:0;', so a legit stored false and a corrupt file are indistinguishable; the corrupt file is also never unlinked (the no-newline path at 579 DOES unlink, so the asymmetry is clear). Severity medium is fair: needs a torn-write window, but the result (wrong false served until TTL, log spam) is real.

Fix assessment: the proposed `@unserialize(...); if ($val === false && $serialized !== 'b:0;') { @unlink; return null; } return $val;` resolves the silent-corruption + log-spam and the 'b:0;' guard correctly preserves a legitimately-stored false. BC/test risk LOW — no existing test stores false to the file tier (file-tier tests use str_repeat('x',8192) strings; the only bool test stores ['nested'=>true] in memory), and suppressing the warning + unlinking torn files changes no documented behavior. GAP: the fix only catches the false-collision; a torn body that deserializes to a different valid-but-wrong value still slips through. The memory tier already crc32-verifies (line 334); the file tier is asymmetrically weaker. The robust fix is the author's optional crc/length-prefix line, mirroring the memory tier — the minimal `false`-guard is a real improvement but not complete.

findings.title: has() returns true for an empty / newline-less cache file while get() returns a miss — has/get disagree
findings.description: has()'s file branch opens the file, does `$ttlLine = (int) fgets($f)` and, if `$ttlLine` is not an expired positive value, returns true. On a zero-byte file fgets() returns false → (int)false = 0 → treated as 'no TTL, alive' → has() returns true. On a file containing just `0` with no newline, fgets returns '0' → 0 → also true. But readFile() for the same file finds no newline (strpos === false) and unlinks + returns null (miss). So has($k) === true while get($k) misses.
