Such files arise from the non-atomic writeFile being interrupted (partial write of only the TTL prefix, or a 0-byte file from a failed/concurrent write).
findings.impact: Violates the cache's own has()/get() coherence contract (and PSR-16 via SimpleCacheAdapter::has, which delegates here): code that guards `if (Cache::has($k)) { $v = Cache::get($k); use($v); }` gets a null/default it didn't expect, or skips a recompute it should have done. Low severity because it requires a partially-written file, but it's a latent correctness gap.
findings.fix: In has(), validate the line structurally like readFile: require fgets() to return a non-false string AND require the file to actually contain a newline-delimited body before declaring a hit; on a malformed file, @unlink and return false so has() and get() agree.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED REAL on current disk. has() file branch (src/Cache.php:396-406): line 400 `$ttlLine = (int) fgets($f)`. Zero-byte file → fgets()=false → (int)false=0 → line 402 `0>0` false → line 406 returns true. File "0" (no newline) → fgets='0' → 0 → true. File "<future-ttl>" (no newline/body) → ttlLine>0 && ttlLine<now is false → true. readFile() (src/Cache.php:565-591): line 577-581 `strpos($content,"
")===false` → @unlink + return null, so get() (line 340-347) returns the default (miss). All three malformed shapes: has()=true while get()=miss → genuine coherence break, also propagated through SimpleCacheAdapter::has() (line 39-42 delegates to Cache::has). Precondition is reachable: writeFile (line 562) is `file_put_contents($path, $expires."
".$serialized)` with NO temp+rename and NO LOCK_EX, so a killed/disk-full/concurrent partial write can leave a TTL-prefix-only or zero-byte file. FIX ASSESSMENT: correct. Mirroring readFile's structural check in has() (require non-false fgets AND a newline; else @unlink + return false) makes has() agree with get(). Cleanest impl reads content and checks `strpos($content,"
")!==false` identical to readFile (the claim's 'fgets + newline' wording works too, just slightly awkward). BC/TEST RISK: none — checked tests/Unit/CacheTest.php, CacheV040Test.php, CacheCoverageTest.php, SimpleCacheAdapterTest.php: no test creates a malformed/empty/newline-less file; every asserted `assertTrue(Cache::has(...))` is on a properly-written file (always has 
). CacheCoverageTest:117 only covers the chmod-000 unreadable path (fopen false → already returns false), untouched by the fix. Severity low is right: needs a corrupt/partial file, fail-safe direction, latent correctness gap not a crash.

findings.title: writeFile is non-atomic (no LOCK_EX, no temp+rename) — concurrent readers can read torn writes; a crash/ENOSPC mid-write leaves a corrupt file
findings.description: writeFile does `file_put_contents($path, $expires."\n".$serialized)` with no flags. file_put_contents on an existing path truncates then writes in chunks; it takes no exclusive lock by default. On a long-lived multi-coroutine/multi-worker server, two set()s for the same key (or a set() racing a readFile()) can interleave so a reader observes a half-written body. Under HOOK_ALL the write itself can yield mid-way between coroutines. A crash or disk-full between truncate and full write leaves a permanently corrupt/short file. This is the upstream cause of the torn-body and empty-file findings above.
findings.impact: Intermittent corrupt file-tier reads (returned as bool false per the readFile finding, or has/get disagreement per the empty-file finding) under concurrency or after a crash. Hard to reproduce, easy to ship.
findings.fix: Write atomically: write to a unique temp file in the same directory then rename() over the target (rename is atomic on POSIX same-filesystem), and/or pass LOCK_EX to file_put_contents. e.g. `$tmp = $path.'.'.getmypid().'.'.bin2hex(random_bytes(4)).'.tmp'; if (file_put_contents($tmp, $data, LOCK_EX) === false) return false; return rename($tmp, $path);` with cleanup of $tmp on failure.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED REAL (current on-disk code). src/Cache.php:559-562: `writeFile()` does `file_put_contents($path, $expires."\n".$serialized)` with NO flags — no LOCK_EX, no temp+rename. file_put_contents on an existing path truncates-then-writes and takes no lock by default, so a concurrent reader (readFile, lines 565-591) or a crash/ENOSPC mid-write can observe a short/torn body. Crucially, the file tier has NO integrity check: the memory tier validates crc32 (set() stores 'crc' at line 247; get() checks it at line 334), but readFile only checks (a) newline presence (line 578) and (b) TTL (line 584) before handing the body straight to `unserialize($serialized, ['allowed_classes'=>false])` at line 590. I confirmed via php -r that unserialize() of truncated/garbage serialized data returns bool(false), which readFile returns directly — so a torn write silently surfaces as a wrong `false` get() value. This IS the genuine upstream cause of the cited "readFile returns false" / "has-get disagreement" findings. Not guarded elsewhere, not intended behavior.

WHY LOW (not medium): (1) The window is narrow and same-key-concurrent. Cache keys are content-addressed by md5(key); two writers tear each other only on the SAME key writing DIFFERENT values simultaneously, and even then the loser still produces a valid (if losing) file — corruption needs an interleave or a real crash/disk-full. (2) HOOK_ALL does NOT coroutinize plain-disk file_put_contents to a regular file (OpenSwoole's stream hook covers sockets/pipes, not blocking regular-file I/O on most setups), so the intra-worker multi-coroutine interleave the claim leans on is weaker than stated; the realistic triggers are multi-WORKER same-key races and process crash/ENOSPC. (3) Self-healing: a torn file is overwritten on the next set() for that key, and gc/del clean stale files. (4) Documented LIMITATION already: the class header (lines 27-34) explicitly says 'Persistence: File tier is best-effort. Redis AOF/RDB is crash-safe.' — so crash-corruption of the file tier is partly acknowledged-by-design, though concurrent torn reads under normal operation are not.

FIX ASSESSMENT: The proposed temp-file + rename() fix is correct and BC-safe. rename() over a same-directory target on the same filesystem is atomic on POSIX, eliminating torn reads (readers see either old or new whole file). LOCK_EX on the unique temp file is harmless. writeFile's bool return contract is preserved. random_bytes/getmypid are always available. No existing test depends on writeFile's internal mechanism: CacheTest writes malformed files DIRECTLY via file_put_contents (line 236-237) to exercise the corrupt-path branch — unaffected. testLargeValueFallsToFileOnly / testFileTierFallback / has() tests only assert the final file exists + content round-trips — all still pass after temp+rename. One refinement to the claim's snippet: it must unlink the temp file on rename() failure to avoid leaking .tmp files (and those .tmp files must not match the gc/maxFiles glob of '*.cache' — the suggested '.tmp' suffix already avoids the '*.cache' pattern, so safe). A defensive belt-and-suspenders alternative — adding a crc to the file tier like the memory tier already has — would also catch crash-corruption that survives rename, but is not required to close the torn-read race. Net: genuine low-severity robustness bug; fix is sound with the temp-cleanup caveat.

findings.title: gcFiles maxFiles trim calls filemtime() after the alive[] list is built — files unlinked/replaced concurrently make sort and unlink act on stale paths (TOCTOU)
findings.description: After expired-file cleanup, the maxFiles trim does `usort($alive, fn($a,$b)=> filemtime($a) <=> filemtime($b))` then `@unlink($alive[$i])`. Between glob() (line 627), the fopen/fgets pass, the filemtime() calls in usort, and the unlink, another worker can have deleted or rewritten any of these files. filemtime() on a since-deleted path emits a warning and returns false (sorted as mtime 0), and the @unlink may remove a file that was just rewritten fresh (evicting a live, newly-set entry instead of the intended oldest). Also @unlink failures are swallowed, so a file that can't be removed is re-counted every sweep.
findings.impact: Under concurrency the oldest-first eviction is approximate and can occasionally drop a just-written hot entry, and can emit filemtime warnings to the log. Low impact (it's a best-effort GC) but the cap can also be transiently violated if unlinks fail silently.
findings.fix: Capture mtime alongside the path during the single fopen pass (the file is already open) so sorting uses a consistent snapshot; guard filemtime with @ / a stat that tolerates missing files; and treat a failed @unlink so the trim loop doesn't assume success. At minimum, fetch filemtime once into a parallel array before usort rather than calling it inside the comparator.
findings.verifyNotes:
PARTIALLY REAL — the genuine, narrow defect is the un-suppressed filemtime() in the usort comparator (line 649), the only FS call in gcFiles() without an @ guard (@fopen line 630, @unlink lines 637/653 all have it). If a .cache file is deleted between glob() (627) and the usort pass — e.g. by another worker's Cache::get() lazy-expiry @unlink (line 585) or a concurrent set() rewrite — filemtime() emits a PHP warning and returns false. Verified on disk at 646-656 exactly as cited.

CONCURRENCY PREMISE — verified but narrower than claimed: gcFiles() runs ONLY from the GC timer (registerGc, line 595-602) gated on `if ($workerId !== 0) return;`, so there is exactly ONE GC sweep per server (worker 0), never two concurrent sweeps. The only concurrent file mutators are normal set()/get() on other workers. So the TOCTOU window exists, but it's sweep-vs-request, not sweep-vs-sweep.

IMPACT OVERSTATED (mostly false positive): (1) filemtime()==false casts to 0 via (int), sorting a vanished path FIRST (oldest) then @unlink'ing it — a harmless no-op, NOT mis-eviction. (2) The "drops a just-written hot entry" claim is essentially wrong: a file rewritten by set() gets a fresh mtime == now, sorting it LAST (newest), so it is NOT in the oldest-$overage eviction window. The delete-then-recreate-between-glob-and-usort race is vanishingly small AND self-heals next sweep. (3) Swallowed @unlink failure re-counting a file is intended best-effort GC (docblock: "bounded disk usage is desired"), and fileRotations still increments — a metric nit, not a correctness bug.

FIX: the proposed fix (capture mtime during the existing fopen pass — the file is already open — and sort on the snapshot) is sound, BC-safe, and silences the warning. It does NOT break testGcFilesEvictsOldestWhenOverMaxFilesCap (CacheFileTierAndStampedeTest) which only asserts count<=cap, nor CacheCoverageTest's skip-unreadable path. Minimal acceptable fix: just `@filemtime()` in the comparator. But this is hardening of a best-effort single-worker GC path, not a correctness fix — severity low at most, leaning cosmetic (a warning under a rare race).

findings.title: Counter on the default Atomic backend silently truncates/wraps at 2^32 — values, increments, and decrements outside [0, 4294967295] are corrupted
findings.location: src/Counter/AtomicBackend.php:21-24,91-114 (and the int-typed public API in src/Counter.php:87-151)
findings.description: OpenSwoole\Atomic is a 32-bit UNSIGNED integer (verified at runtime: new Atomic(0)->set(PHP_INT_MAX) reads back 4294967295; set(5_000_000_000) reads back 705032704; add(PHP_INT_MAX) then add(1) wraps to 0; sub(1) from 0 underflows to 4294967295). AtomicBackend wraps it directly via atomic()/add()/sub()/set()/cmpset(). The Counter facade (src/Counter.php) exposes a PHP `int` (64-bit signed) contract: increment(int), set(int), compareAndSet(int,int), and incrementBounded(by, $maxBound = PHP_INT_MAX). So: (1) any counter exceeding ~4.29 billion silently wraps to 0; (2) decrement() below 0 underflows to ~4.29 billion instead of going negative; (3) set()/compareAndSet() with any value >= 2^32 store a truncated value; (4) the RedisCounterBackend uses true 64-bit signed INCRBY/DECRBY, so the SAME logical Counter operation produces DIFFERENT results on 'atomic' vs 'redis' — a backend-inconsistency landmine for anyone who flips Counter::defaultBackend('redis') expecting parity.
findings.impact: Silent data corruption on the default, recommended backend. A high-traffic hit/byte/request counter that crosses 4.29 billion (entirely reachable on a long-lived worker fleet) wraps to 0 with no error. Any counter used for monotonic IDs, quota/byte accounting, or as a CAS guard breaks. decrement() producing a huge positive underflow can defeat ConcurrencyLimit/RateLimit logic built on Counter. Backends that are documented as drop-in interchangeable disagree numerically.
findings.fix: Document and enforce the 32-bit unsigned range as a hard contract OR stop using a single Atomic per counter for 64-bit values. Minimal: in AtomicBackend, guard set()/incr()/decr()/compareAndSet()/incrBounded() against values outside [0, 0xFFFFFFFF] and throw StoreException (mirroring the Redis-only raw() exception pattern) instead of silently wrapping; reject negative results in decr()/incrBounded(). Proper: store 64-bit counters as a pair of Atomics or back them with a small OpenSwoole\Table TYPE_INT (true 64-bit) so the Atomic and Redis backends agree. At minimum, fix the misleading default `incrementBounded(by, PHP_INT_MAX)` cap — PHP_INT_MAX is unreachable on Atomic, so the cap never fires before the value wraps.
findings.verifyNotes:
REAL bug, verified independently on disk + at runtime on this host.

Runtime proof (this machine's OpenSwoole): `new Atomic(0)` then set(PHP_INT_MAX)→reads 4294967295; set(5_000_000_000)→705032704; add(PHP_INT_MAX)→4294967295; 0xFFFFFFFF+add(1)→0; sub(1) from 0→4294967295; cmpset(0, 5_000_000_000)→true then reads 705032704. So OpenSwoole\Atomic is a 32-bit UNSIGNED slot.

Code path confirmed: AtomicBackend (src/Counter/AtomicBackend.php) wraps Atomic directly — get/set/add/sub/cmpset, no range guard (lines 21-24,35-44,91-114). Counter facade (src/Counter.php:87-151) declares a PHP `int` (64-bit signed) contract: increment(int), set(int), compareAndSet(int,int), incrementBounded(int $by, int $maxBound = PHP_INT_MAX). So values >2^32-1 truncate, decrements below 0 underflow to ~4.29e9 instead of negative, and set/cmpset store truncated values — all silent, on the DEFAULT backend.

Parity claim confirmed: RedisCounterBackend (src/Counter/RedisCounterBackend.php:85-92) uses native incrby/decrby = true 64-bit signed. Same Counter call → different result on 'atomic' vs 'redis'. defaultBackend() is documented drop-in interchangeable (src/Counter.php:16-37,184-198). Genuine cross-backend numeric divergence.

incrBounded sub-claim is the strongest part: at AtomicBackend.php:61-74 the guard is `$next = $cur + $by; if ($next > $maxBound) return null`. With the default $maxBound = PHP_INT_MAX (src/Counter.php:135), PHP-side `$cur + $by` is 64-bit so the `> PHP_INT_MAX` test NEVER fires before the value is written to the Atomic and wraps. The default cap is genuinely unreachable — the cap fires only for small explicit bounds (which the N1ToN4 tests pin: 3, 10). So the 'misleading default cap' is real. (One nuance: the truncation happens at the cmpset write, not in the PHP arithmetic — the description's framing is correct in effect.)

Already-guarded check: NO range guard anywhere. Searched src/, docs/, template/ — the 32-bit range is NEVER disclosed. learn/store.php:108 only says 'wraps OpenSwoole\Atomic, lock-free' with no range caveat. No existing test asserts range/wrap (CounterTest, CounterN1ToN4Test, CounterFacadeParityTest all use tiny values). So not intended/documented behavior.

Consumer impact is real but bounded: ConcurrencyLimitMiddleware/RateLimitMiddleware use increment/decrement on counts that realistically never cross 4.29e9 concurrently, so the practical break is monotonic-ID / cumulative byte / total-hit counters on long-lived workers, and any app flipping to Redis expecting parity. Hence HIGH not CRITICAL: silent corruption is severe, but the reachable-in-practice trigger (a single logical counter crossing 4.29 billion) is narrower than 'critical/imminent'; the int-typed API promising 64-bit is the core defect.

Proposed fix assessment: The 'minimal' fix (guard set/incr/decr/compareAndSet/incrBounded against [0, 0xFFFFFFFF], throw StoreException, reject negative decr results) RESOLVES the silent-corruption surface and mirrors the existing raw()-throws-on-redis pattern. BC RISK: throwing on out-of-range is a behavior change but only for inputs that were ALREADY silently corrupting — no correct program relied on the wrap, and no existing test exercises >2^32 (verified), so the unit/integration suite stays green. Rejecting negative decr() results is a semantic choice that could surprise code intentionally using underflow, but no such usage exists in-tree. The 'proper' fix (back with OpenSwoole\Table TYPE_INT = true 64-bit, or paired Atomics) achieves real atomic-vs-redis parity and is the better long-term fix, but is a larger change (CAS semantics on a Table cell need care). Fixing the misleading PHP_INT_MAX default cap (e.g. default to 0xFFFFFFFF on the Atomic backend, or document it) is correct and low-risk. Overall: fix is sound and does not break existing tests; pick the Table-backed 'proper' variant if true backend parity is the goal, else the throw-guard is a safe minimal landing.

findings.title: incrementBounded disagrees between backends on negative results: Redis maps any value <0 to null ("cap exceeded"), Atomic wraps it to a huge unsigned value
findings.description: RedisCounterBackend::incrBounded() uses -1 as the over-cap sentinel in Lua (`if nxt > maxBound then return -1`), but the Lua otherwise returns `nxt` unfloored, and the PHP wrapper at line 64 collapses EVERY negative result to null: `return ($v === null || $v < 0) ? null : $v;`. incrementBounded() accepts `int $by` with no positivity constraint (the Counter docblock examples only show positive deltas, but the signature and the interface allow any int). So incrementBounded(-5, 100) from a current value of 4 computes nxt = -1, which is a LEGITIMATE new value, but the wrapper reports null — i.e. it falsely signals 'cap would be exceeded' when in fact the counter was (or should be) decremented. The Atomic backend (AtomicBackend.php:67-73) has no negative-result collapse — it computes $next = $cur + $by and only checks `$next > $maxBound`, so it returns the (32-bit-wrapped) value instead of null. The two backends therefore return different things (null vs a number) for the identical incrementBounded() call with a negative delta or any path that produces a sub-zero result.
findings.impact: Callers using incrementBounded for slot/quota accounting that can also release slots (negative delta) get inconsistent, wrong answers depending on backend. On Redis a real decrement-to-negative is misreported as 'full' (null); on Atomic the same op silently underflows to ~4.29 billion. Logic that branches on `=== null` (the documented 'pool full' pattern) misfires.
findings.fix: Decide the contract for incrBounded: if it is increment-only, validate `$by >= 0` and reject negatives loudly in Counter::incrementBounded(). If negative deltas are allowed, stop overloading -1/`<0` as the over-cap sentinel — return a distinct out-of-band signal from Lua (e.g. return a 2-element array [status, value], or use a sentinel that cannot collide with a real counter value such as a non-numeric reply) and make the Atomic backend mirror exactly the same semantics (including any lower-bound floor).
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED on disk. Core claim is TRUE: RedisCounterBackend::incrBounded (src/Counter/RedisCounterBackend.php:64) collapses EVERY negative result to null via `($v === null || $v < 0) ? null : $v`, while AtomicBackend (lines 67-73) and MemcachedCounterBackend (lines 132-155) have no negative-collapse and return the actual negative value. The over-cap sentinel `-1` (Lua line 57) collides with a legitimate result of -1. Signature `incrementBounded(int $by, int $maxBound)` (Counter.php:135) + the interface (CounterBackend.php:39) place NO positivity constraint; the interface docblock's only documented failure mode is 'cap would be exceeded', so a negative $by is in-contract. With current=4, incrBounded(-5,100): Redis nxt=-1, not >100, so it SETs key=-1 then returns -1, which PHP collapses to null; Atomic/Memcached return -1. So the three backends disagree (null vs -1).

WORSE THAN CLAIMED: on Redis the Lua `SET KEYS[1] nxt` (line 58) runs BEFORE the PHP collapse — for a negative-but-under-cap result the key IS mutated to the negative value AND null is returned. That's a write-then-report-failure (stored state diverges from the return contract), not merely a misreported no-op. The claim's fix doesn't call this out.

CLAIM INACCURACIES: 'Atomic wraps it to a huge unsigned value (~4.29 billion)' / '32-bit-wrapped' is WRONG — OpenSwoole\\Atomic is signed 64-bit; it returns -1, not a huge unsigned. Claim also omits the 3rd backend (Memcached), which behaves like Atomic.

SEVERITY medium not high: zero production callers use negative deltas (grep: no callers outside tests); design intent is increment-only slot/quota (docblock examples + the -1 over-cap sentinel assume monotonic-up); triggering needs a negative $by passed to an increment-named API. Latent public-API edge-case, not an actively-firing bug.

FIX: The increment-only path (validate $by >= 0 in Counter::incrementBounded, reject negatives) is BC-safe — all existing tests (CounterN1ToN4Test, RedisCounterBackendFullTest, MemcachedCounterBackendTest) use positive deltas and assert null only on genuine over-cap, so none break. It matches the evident design intent and is the cleaner resolution. The allow-negatives path (redesign the Lua sentinel to an out-of-band signal + mirror lower-bound semantics across all 3 backends + stop SET-before-null on Redis) is more invasive but also doesn't break the existing positive-only tests. Either resolves the divergence; the Redis SET-before-collapse should be fixed regardless.

findings.title: AtomicBackend::incrBounded returns null on CAS-retry exhaustion — indistinguishable from a legitimate 'cap exceeded' null
findings.description: incrBounded loops 100 times on cmpset; on contention-loss it `return null;` (line 73) — the SAME sentinel it returns for the genuine over-cap case (line 70). A caller using the documented 'if ($slots->incrementBounded(1,100) === null) return "pool full";' pattern cannot distinguish 'the cap was actually hit' from 'we lost the CAS race 100 times and gave up even though capacity remained'. Under heavy contention on a hot counter the bound check spuriously reports 'full' and rejects a request that should have succeeded. The Redis backend has no such retry-exhaustion path (server-side Lua is single-shot), so again the two backends behave differently under contention.
findings.impact: Spurious 503/'pool full' rejections under high concurrency on the Atomic backend even when the counter is below its cap. Silent because no error/log is emitted on exhaustion.
findings.fix: Differentiate the two failure modes: throw a StoreException (or a dedicated retriable signal) on CAS-retry exhaustion instead of returning the cap-exceeded null, OR raise the retry ceiling and log when exhausted. Document that null strictly means 'cap exceeded' so the over-cap branch is reliable.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED on disk. src/Counter/AtomicBackend.php:61-74 matches the claim exactly: line 70 returns null for genuine over-cap, line 73 returns null for 100-attempt CAS-retry exhaustion — same sentinel, indistinguishable. The documented contract reinforces the conflation: Counter.php:125-138 states null means "the cap would be exceeded" and the canonical example treats null as "pool full" → reject. So a caller following the documented pattern WOULD misread an exhaustion-null as a real cap hit. Cross-backend claim is correct but understated: RedisCounterBackend (Lua, single-shot) has no exhaustion path; but MemcachedCounterBackend (line 132-155) has the IDENTICAL 100-attempt CAS loop returning null on exhaustion — so it is two backends, not just Atomic, that diverge from Redis.

REACHABILITY (why low is correct, arguably even lower): Atomic::cmpset is a C-level lock-free hardware CAS; one get+cmpset iteration is nanoseconds. OpenSwoole coroutines are non-preemptive and yield only at I/O boundaries — there is NO I/O between get() and cmpset(), so within a single worker the loop effectively never loses. Exhausting all 100 attempts requires 100 consecutive losing CAS races (100 competing successful writes interleaving) — astronomically unlikely under realistic load. Crucially, the framework's own hot path (ConcurrencyLimitMiddleware) does NOT use incrementBounded — it uses plain increment(1)/decrement(1) with manual rollback (lines 200/253). incrBounded is only exercised by tests/docs/user-code. So there is zero in-tree spurious-503 exposure; only hypothetical user code is affected.

FIX ASSESSMENT: The proposed primary fix (throw StoreException on exhaustion) is a BC BREAK — it changes the documented return contract (null-only) and would break the documented "=== null → pool full" example plus any caller not catching the exception; it also can't be mirrored on the Redis backend (no loop to exhaust), widening divergence. The claim's ALTERNATIVE (raise the retry ceiling and/or log on exhaustion, and document that null strictly means cap-exceeded) is non-breaking and does not affect existing tests (they only assert genuine over-cap → null, unaffected). That alternative is the correct resolution. Net: genuine latent ambiguity, but the harmful manifestation is practically unreachable given the non-preemptive coroutine model + nanosecond CAS, and the framework never hits the path — low severity is appropriate.

findings.title: dispatchRawRoute ExitException handler drains the ENTIRE process-global output-buffer stack, not just its own frame
findings.description: In the raw-route dispatch path, the ExitException catch does `while (ob_get_level() > 0) { $buffered = (string)ob_get_clean() . $buffered; }` — it pops EVERY output buffer currently on PHP's process-global ob stack, not just buffers this dispatch opened. Raw routes deliberately do NOT call ob_start() themselves, so any buffer present at exit was opened by an OUTER layer or, under coroutine concurrency, by a DIFFERENT coroutine. PHP's output-buffer stack is process-global and is explicitly NOT isolated per coroutine (CLAUDE.md: "raw ob_* ... not specifically isolation-tested under concurrency"). If coroutine A is mid-handler with an open ob_start() buffer (the normal dispatchMatched path opens one at line 298) and yields on hooked I/O, and coroutine B runs a raw route that hits exit()/die() (ExitException), B's drain loop will ob_get_clean() A's buffer too — stealing/clearing A's pending output and corrupting A's response.
findings.impact: Under coroutine concurrency, an exit()/die() inside a raw route can swallow or corrupt another in-flight coroutine's buffered response body (truncated/empty/cross-contaminated output). Hard to reproduce, data-dependent, and silent — exactly the class of cross-request bug the framework's isolation work targets, but ob_* is an acknowledged gap.
findings.fix: Capture the baseline ob level before the handler runs and only drain down to it. Since dispatchRawRoute opens no buffer, record `$obBase = ob_get_level();` at the top of the try (before call_user_func_array) and bound the drain: `while (ob_get_level() > $obBase) { $buffered = (string)ob_get_clean() . $buffered; }`. This never touches buffers owned by outer layers or other coroutines. Apply the same baseline-bounded pattern anywhere a `while (ob_get_level() > 0)` drain exists.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED on disk. src/ResponseMiddleware.php:130-138 — in dispatchRawRoute's catch, the ExitException branch does `$buffered=''; while (ob_get_level() > 0) { $buffered = (string)ob_get_clean() . $buffered; }`. The drain bound is `> 0`, i.e. it pops EVERY buffer on PHP's OB stack, not just buffers this dispatch owns.

Premises all confirmed independently:
1. Raw routes open NO buffer of their own — dispatchRawRoute calls call_user_func_array (line 66) with no preceding ob_start(), unlike dispatchMatched which does ob_start() at line 298. docs/routing.md:44 documents `raw` = "skip the per-request output buffer (ob_start())". So any buffer present at exit was NOT opened by this code path.
2. PHP's OB stack is process-global and explicitly NOT isolated per coroutine in this codebase: ext/zealphp/README.md:51 lists "raw ob_*" under "Not isolated (by design)"; docs/architecture/2026-05-31-sapi-contract-coverage.md:84,150 same. The ext only overrides flush funcs (ob_flush/ob_end_flush), never snapshots the OB stack on yield/resume.
3. Interleave is real: dispatchMatched (line 298) opens ob_start() then the handler can yield on hooked I/O (HOOK_ALL default in coroutine mode) while that buffer is open; a concurrent coroutine B running a raw route that hits exit()/die() (ExitException) would ob_get_clean() coroutine A's still-open buffer → steals/clears A's pending output.
4. The framework already KNOWS the correct pattern: App::executeFile() (src/App.php:5270 + 5310) records `$obBase=ob_get_level()` and drains only `while (ob_get_level() > $obBase + 1)`. dispatchRawRoute is the inconsistent outlier.

The fix is correct and exactly mirrors the executeFile precedent: capture `$obBase=ob_get_level()` at the top of the try (before call_user_func_array) and bound the drain to `> $obBase`. BC-safe: raw routes open no buffer, so in the normal single-request flow (process() does NOT wrap dispatch in ob_start; CoSessionManager/SessionManager open no buffer) the stack is empty at entry → $obBase=0 → drain stays a no-op just like today; any buffer the handler itself opened-but-didn't-close before exit is still drained (>$obBase catches it). No existing test exercises raw+exit+buffers (testRawRouteThrow uses a plain RuntimeException, not exit), so no test breakage.

Note: the sibling non-raw catch at line 380 does a SINGLE ob_get_clean() (correct — it owns exactly the one buffer dispatchMatched opened), so only the raw path has the unbounded drain. Severity LOW not medium: trigger requires the narrow conjunction of coroutine mode + a raw route (these are stream/direct-$response paths where exit() is atypical) + an exit()/die() in it + concurrent overlap with a buffered non-raw handler that yields mid-buffer; data-corruption is silent but the preconditions rarely co-occur in practice. Real defect with a clean, low-risk fix; worth applying for correctness + consistency with executeFile.

findings.title: $g->psr_request stash is set every request but never cleared; in superglobals mode (process-wide singleton) it pins the prior request's full PSR-7 object + body stream
findings.location: src/ResponseMiddleware.php:443 (set); src/Session/SessionManager.php:202-213 (reset list that omits it)
findings.description: process() does `$g->psr_request = $request;` on every request (ResponseMiddleware.php:443) but nothing ever nulls it. In coroutine mode RequestContext lives on Coroutine::getContext() and is freed when the coroutine ends, so the reference dies naturally. In superglobals/legacy mode RequestContext is a PROCESS-WIDE singleton; SessionManager's per-request reset block (SessionManager.php:202-213) was added precisely to stop process-wide slots leaking across requests, and it explicitly resets status/_streaming/error stacks/etc. — but psr_request is NOT in that list. Because process() overwrites it before any consumer reads it, there is no correctness bug (ZealAPI.php:313 always sees the current request). But between request N's end and request N+1's process() call, the singleton retains a strong reference to request N's entire ServerRequestInterface, including its buffered body — so on an idle worker the previous request's request object (and its body memory) stays pinned until the next request replaces it.
findings.impact: Minor: one stale large request body held alive on each idle superglobals-mode worker between requests; bounded (single object, replaced next request), not unbounded growth. Also a faint cross-request information-retention smell — a debugging/introspection read of $g->psr_request between requests would see the prior request.
findings.fix: Add `$g->psr_request = null;` to the SessionManager (and CoSessionManager, for symmetry) per-request reset block alongside the existing status/_streaming resets, so the singleton drops the reference at request end.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED ON DISK. (1) ResponseMiddleware.php:443 unconditionally sets `$g->psr_request = $request;` every request in process(). (2) grep across src/, app.php, route/, api/ confirms only 3 sites: set (443), read (ZealAPI.php:313-314), declaration (RequestContext.php:70) — NEVER assigned null anywhere. (3) SessionManager.php:202-213 reset block resets error/exception/shutdown stacks, status, _streaming, etc. but OMITS psr_request; the block's own comment (195-201) confirms it exists precisely to stop process-wide-singleton slots leaking across requests. (4) RequestContext::instance() (107-125) returns the process-wide self::$instance when App::$superglobals && !coroutine_isolated, so the slot persists across requests on a worker. (5) The reset block runs regardless of sessionLifecycle (gated only on instance()), so it's the correct symmetric home for the fix.

CORRECTNESS: No bug — process() overwrites psr_request before any consumer reads (ZealAPI.php:313 always sees current request). The only effect is between request N end and N+1 process(): the singleton retains a strong ref to request N's ServerRequestInterface. Real-but-trivial resource/info-retention hygiene issue, severity low as the reporter concedes — bounded single-object (replaced next request), not unbounded growth. Note: the request uses LazyServerRequest (hydrates lazily), so the pinned object is the wrapper around OpenSwoole's request, not necessarily a fully materialized buffered body — slightly lessens the already-minor memory impact, but the strong reference (and the introspection-read smell) is genuine.

FIX: Adding `$g->psr_request = null;` to the SessionManager reset block is correct, safe, and BC-preserving — it nulls a slot always overwritten before read, so no consumer can observe the change; cannot break existing tests. CoSessionManager doesn't strictly need it (per-coroutine context frees naturally on coroutine end), but adding for symmetry is harmless. Fix resolves the stated issue without risk.

findings.title: phpredis PSUBSCRIBE coroutine + its Redis connection leak on every stop() — stop sentinel only reaches the exact-channel socket
findings.location: src/Store/RedisPubSub.php:147 (sentinel only in $exacts) + src/Store/PhpredisDriver.php:543-557 (separate psubscribe coroutine/client)
findings.description: RedisPubSub::runner() builds the stop sentinel into the EXACT channel set only: `$exacts = array_merge(array_keys($this->exactHandlers), [$this->stopChannel])` (line 147), while `$patterns = array_keys($this->patternHandlers)` (line 148). PhpredisDriver::subscribe() then runs SUBSCRIBE and PSUBSCRIBE in two SEPARATE coroutines, each owning its OWN \Redis client (lines 525-557). On stop(), `RedisPubSub::stop()` does `PUBLISH $stopChannel '__stop__'` — that message is only delivered to the EXACT subscriber socket. The PSUBSCRIBE coroutine never matches `$stopChannel` (it is a literal channel, not one of the registered `*` patterns), so its callback never runs, never sees `$running===0`, and the phpredis `->psubscribe()` call blocks forever C-side. The pattern coroutine and its dedicated Redis connection are orphaned. This happens on EVERY clean stop whenever any pattern handler is registered (e.g. the demo's `App::subscribe('demo:pubsub:*', ...)`). On a long-lived worker that recycles, each recycle leaks one Redis connection + one stuck coroutine.
findings.impact: Every worker stop/recycle with a registered PSUBSCRIBE pattern leaks one Redis connection (fd) and one zombie coroutine that never exits cleanly; the psubscribe socket is only freed when the TCP connection eventually drops. Over many worker recycles this accumulates connections against the Redis maxclients budget and prevents truly clean shutdown. The driver-level RedisException catch (line 553) only fires on an actual socket drop, not on the clean-stop path.
findings.fix: Make stop() reach BOTH sockets: register a stop PATTERN as well as a stop channel. Either (a) add a sentinel pattern such as `$this->stopChannel.':*'` (or reuse `$this->stopChannel` as a PSUBSCRIBE pattern target) to `$patterns` in runner() and PUBLISH to a name that matches it in stop(), so the psubscribe coroutine also receives the sentinel; or (b) have PhpredisDriver::subscribe() close the psubscribe client when the exact coroutine sees the stop (share the `$running` Atomic, and after the outer loop breaks, force-close both clients in the finally at line 571-573 so the blocked psubscribe socket is torn down). Simplest robust fix: in PhpredisDriver::subscribe()'s `finally`, explicitly `$client->close()` the psubscribe coroutine's client (track both clients) to unblock the C-side read.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED REAL on current on-disk code. Mechanism traced end-to-end:

- RedisPubSub::runner() (src/Store/RedisPubSub.php:147-148) puts the stop sentinel into $exacts only: `$exacts = array_merge(array_keys($this->exactHandlers), [$this->stopChannel])`; `$patterns = array_keys($this->patternHandlers)`. Correct as claimed.
- PhpredisDriver::subscribe() (src/Store/PhpredisDriver.php:525-557) spawns TWO independent go() coroutines, each building its OWN \Redis via buildClient() (lines 528, 546) and blocking in C-side ->subscribe()/->psubscribe(). The inner $client locals are never closed — confirmed: grep found zero `$client->close()` inside subscribe(); the finally (571-573) only does `$running->set(0)`. RedisPubSub::runner line 158 `$client->close()` closes only the driver's MAIN connection, not the two inner sockets.
- On clean stop(): stop() PUBLISHes `__stop__` to $stopChannel (an exact literal channel). It reaches only the SUBSCRIBE socket. The psubscribe callback never matches a literal channel against a `*` pattern, so it never fires, never checks the shared $running Atomic, and ->psubscribe() blocks forever C-side. The pattern coroutine + its dedicated \Redis connection are orphaned on every clean stop whenever a pattern handler is registered.

Trigger is real and exercised: route/demo.php:274 registers `App::subscribe('demo:pubsub:*', ...)`; App::wirePubSubBoot (src/App.php:4760-4778) spawns one RedisPubSub per worker and wires `pubsub->stop()` into onWorkerStop. So every worker recycle/graceful-stop leaks one connection (fd) + one zombie coroutine until the TCP connection eventually drops server-side.

Two accuracy refinements to the claim (do not change the verdict): (1) The exact-channel coroutine ALSO leaks on clean stop, not just the pattern one — when the stop frame's callback runs (line 531) the driver-level $running is still 1 (it only flips at line 569 AFTER the consumer throws), so the exact coroutine re-blocks too and never re-checks $running. The claim's pattern-only framing understates the leak slightly but is directionally correct; the pattern path is the unambiguous case. (2) Severity: downgraded high→medium. It's a genuine fd/coroutine leak per recycle, but (a) leaks are one-per-recycle not one-per-request, (b) the OS/Redis eventually reclaims on connection idle/timeout, (c) phpredis is only the non-default driver — PredisDriver::subscribe() (lines 313-340) uses a SINGLE pubSubLoop with no second coroutine and is NOT affected, and (d) `App::subscribe(... '*' ...)` with phpredis+HOOK_ALL is a relatively narrow production config.

PROPOSED FIX assessment: Option (b) — capture both inner clients by reference and `->close()` them in subscribe()'s finally — is correct and minimal. Closing the socket forces the blocked C-side ->subscribe()/->psubscribe() to return/throw RedisException, which is ALREADY caught at lines 535 and 553 (the existing comments literally anticipate 'once the subscribe socket closes'), so it composes cleanly. A null-guard handles the benign race where finally runs before an inner coroutine assigned its client. Option (a) — adding a sentinel PATTERN — also works but is uglier. BC/TEST RISK: none. No existing test asserts inner-coroutine teardown — testStopHaltsTheRunner (RedisPubSubTest:123) registers only an exact channel and asserts isRunning() (the OUTER Atomic, flipped synchronously by stop()); testPatternHandlerCatchesMatchingPublish asserts only message receipt, never that the pattern coroutine exited. The leak fails no assertion today, and deterministically closing on stop changes none of those observable behaviours. PHPStan-clean fix is feasible (by-ref capture + isset/null check). Recommend fixing with option (b), and a regression test would need fd-counting or coroutine-count assertions to actually pin it.

findings.title: RedisStreams idle stop-latency and message-pickup latency scale with consumer count (sequential per-consumer BLOCK)
findings.location: src/Store/RedisStreams.php:87-97 (foreach consumer: one blocking xreadGroup each, sequentially)
findings.description: The runner iterates every registered consumer sequentially, issuing a separate `XREADGROUP ... BLOCK $blockMs` per consumer (lines 89-92). When all streams are idle, one full cycle blocks up to `sum(blockMs over all consumers)` — with the default 1000ms and, say, 4 distinct streams/groups that is up to 4s per cycle. The stop flag is only checked at the top of each consumer iteration (line 88), so RedisStreams::stop() can take up to one full idle cycle (worst case ~N×blockMs) to take effect, and a message arriving on consumer[3] right after consumer[0]'s block started waits behind consumer[1] and [2]'s full block windows before being read.
findings.impact: With multiple registered streams, worst-case message delivery latency and graceful-stop latency grow linearly with the number of distinct consumers (N × blockMs), not the documented single blockMs cadence. For a few high-blockMs streams this manifests as multi-second pickup delays and slow worker shutdown, undermining the 'reliable' path's responsiveness.
findings.fix: Group consumers sharing the same group/blockMs/batchSize into a single multi-stream XREADGROUP (the code's own comment at lines 66-71/84-86 acknowledges this is unimplemented), so one BLOCK covers all same-group streams. For genuinely different groups, run each in its own go() coroutine so their BLOCK windows overlap instead of serializing. Also re-check the stop atomic inside the message loop, not only at the top of each consumer iteration.
findings.verifyNotes:
VERIFIED on-disk at src/Store/RedisStreams.php:72-111. The runner's foreach (lines 87-97) iterates every registered consumer sequentially, issuing one blocking xreadGroup(group, consumer, [stream], batchSize, blockMs) per consumer (lines 89-92). The driver confirms BLOCK semantics: PhpredisDriver::xreadGroup (line 628) passes $blockMs straight to ext-redis xReadGroup, which blocks up to $blockMs ms when the stream is idle. So with N idle consumers one cycle does block up to sum(blockMs) — N=4 × default blockMs=1000 (register() default, line 41) ≈ 4s/cycle. The stop atomic is checked only at the OUTER while (line 77) and at the TOP of each consumer iteration (line 88) — never inside the per-message dispatch loop (94-96) and never mid-block — so stop() can take up to ~N×blockMs to take effect worst case. The claim's reasoning is accurate. Reachability: App::subscribeReliable (App.php:4663) registers one consumer per call (App.php:4783), so multiple distinct streams accumulate as N separate consumers in normal use — the multi-stream scenario is real, not contrived.

BUT this is intended/acknowledged behavior, not an undetected defect. The class docstring (lines 18-20) frames blockMs as "the natural wake-up cadence," and the runner docblock (66-71) + inline comment (84-86) EXPLICITLY state grouping same-group streams is unimplemented ("Could be optimised by grouping same-group streams but groups are typically per-stream in practice"). It is a latency/throughput degradation under a multi-stream config, NOT a correctness bug: messages are still delivered, XACK still works, stop() still terminates. The 5 existing tests (RedisStreamsTest.php) all use a SINGLE registered consumer, so none pin or contradict multi-consumer cadence.

Fix correctness/BC: the proposed fix is feasible — the driver xreadGroup already accepts array $streams, so same-(group,blockMs,batchSize) consumers can be merged into one multi-stream XREADGROUP; genuinely different groups can run in their own go() so BLOCK windows overlap; and re-checking the stop atomic inside the dispatch loop is trivial. None of this breaks BC or any existing test (all single-consumer). Marking real=true with severity low because the description is factually correct on current code, but reviewers should weigh that the authors documented this as a known, deliberate simplification rather than an oversight.

findings.title: H7 phpredis+HOOK_ALL=0 SUBSCRIBE-deadlock condition is only WARNED, then the worker is still wired to deadlock
findings.location: src/App.php:4736-4746 (redisBootChecks warns) + src/App.php:4771-4787 (wirePubSubBoot starts the runner regardless)
findings.description: redisBootChecks() correctly detects the unsafe combo — pub/sub subscribers registered + HOOK_ALL==0 + phpredis driver — and emits a warning string (lines 4742-4745). But that is purely advisory: it is printed to error_log at boot (App.php:6911-6913) and nothing prevents wirePubSubBoot() from starting the RedisPubSub runner in onWorkerStart anyway (lines 4771-4778). Under that combo the phpredis C-side SUBSCRIBE read blocks the ENTIRE worker (it is not yield-able without HOOK_ALL), so the worker's go(runner) never returns control to the event loop and the worker stops serving HTTP requests. The CLAUDE.md notes phpredis SUBSCRIBE blocks without HOOK_ALL, yet the runner is still launched.
findings.impact: A user who disables HOOK_ALL (testing, custom hooks, or coroutine-without-HOOK_ALL mode) and relies on the default phpredis driver gets a SILENTLY DEADLOCKED worker that no longer serves requests — only a warning in the log distinguishes it from a hang. The guard detects the exact failure but does not avert it.
findings.fix: When redisBootChecks() detects the H7 combo, either (a) auto-force the predis driver for the subscriber runner (predis SUBSCRIBE yields under HOOK_ALL/without it via pure-PHP socket), e.g. construct RedisPubSub/RedisStreams clients with ['prefer'=>'predis'] in that case; or (b) refuse to start the runner and throw/error loudly rather than spawn a worker-blocking coroutine; or (c) at minimum gate wirePubSubBoot's start() on the check and skip spawning with a fatal-level log. Detection without mitigation leaves a foot-gun armed.
findings.verifyNotes:
REAL. Verified independently on disk. (1) Detection-only: redisBootChecks() (App.php:4710-4748) only appends a warning string; run() (App.php:6911-6913) only error_log()s it. (2) Unconditional wiring: wirePubSubBoot() (App.php:4755-4789) registers an onWorkerStart hook that calls $pubsub->start()/$streams->start() with ZERO consultation of the H7 condition — there is no gate. (3) Reachable: validateLifecycleCombination() (App.php:3075-3105) does NOT refuse superglobals(false)+enableCoroutine(true)+hookAll(0) — that is the explicitly-supported "Coroutine without HOOK_ALL" mode, so a user can land in exactly the warned state. The blocking premise (phpredis SUBSCRIBE blocks the worker without HOOK_ALL) is the project's OWN documented claim (H7 docblock App.php:4702-4705 + CLAUDE.md); I did not re-derive OpenSwoole C internals but the framework built this guard specifically around it.

ADDITIONAL finding the claim under-states (strengthens the bug): the warning's OWN suggested workaround — ZEALPHP_REDIS_PREFER=predis — does not actually reach the runner. RedisPubSub/RedisStreams build their dedicated SUBSCRIBE connection via bare `new RedisClient($this->url)` (RedisPubSub.php:125,146; RedisStreams.php:80,147), which defaults prefer to 'auto' and never reads ZEALPHP_REDIS_PREFER (only Store.php:227 + Counter.php:254 consume that env, for the pool/counter — not the subscriber socket). pickDriver('auto') picks phpredis whenever ext-redis is loaded (RedisClient.php:50-51). So even a user who follows the advice keeps a phpredis subscriber and still deadlocks.

FIX assessment: directionally correct. Option (a) auto-force predis is the right one but needs MORE scope than the claim implies — RedisPubSub/RedisStreams constructors don't accept a prefer hint today, so they'd have to be extended to thread prefer into their internal new RedisClient(...) calls, and wirePubSubBoot would pass 'predis' when the H7 combo is detected. Option (c) one-line gate (skip start + fatal-log) is the minimal safe fix. BC risk LOW (only triggers in the otherwise-deadlocking combo; auto-forcing predis is behavior-preserving for everyone else). Test risk LOW — existing AppRedisBootChecksTest only asserts the warning strings (the runner wiring is integration territory and not unit-tested), so a gate/force change wouldn't break it; add a new test for the gating/forcing. Severity medium: requires a non-default config (HOOK_ALL explicitly off) + phpredis + a registered subscriber, but when hit the worker silently stops serving HTTP — the guard detects the exact failure yet leaves the foot-gun armed.
